→ 1	# GitHub as a Second Fiction Source — Design
→ 2	
→ 3	**Status:** Draft, awaiting JP approval before implementation.
→ 4	**Author:** Claude (storyvox session, 2026-05-06).
→ 5	
→ 6	## Goal
→ 7	
→ 8	Let storyvox treat a GitHub repo as a fiction the same way it treats a Royal Road `/fiction/{id}` URL today: add it, see chapters, listen with sentence highlighting, sync new chapters as commits land. Royal Road remains the primary source; GitHub is the next plugin.
→ 9	
→ 10	## Why GitHub specifically
→ 11	
→ 12	- Some web-fiction authors already mirror or originally publish on GitHub (mdbook-style repos, plain markdown chapters).
→ 13	- Free, no anti-bot wall (storyvox already has to fight Cloudflare for RR).
→ 14	- Public API gives us commit history → "new chapter" detection is just a `compare` call instead of polling HTML.
→ 15	- Plays well with the realm aesthetic ("the Library accepts new tomes from any forge").
→ 16	
→ 17	## Non-goals (v0.4)
→ 18	
→ 19	- Private repos — defer until a clean GitHub OAuth path exists.
→ 20	- Voice-tagging from manifest (`narrator: "en-US-Andrew"`) — interesting later, not now.
→ 21	- ePub/PDF/Gutenberg ingest — out of scope.
→ 22	
→ 23	## Architecture
→ 24	
→ 25	### Multi-source refactor (prerequisite)
→ 26	
→ 27	The current data layer assumes one source: `FictionRepository` takes `private val source: FictionSource`. Adding GitHub forces us to route per-fiction by `sourceId`.
→ 28	
→ 29	**Change:**
→ 30	
→ 31	```kotlin
→ 32	// :core-data
→ 33	@Inject constructor(
→ 34	    sources: Map<String, @JvmSuppressWildcards FictionSource>,
→ 35	    // ...
→ 36	) {
→ 37	    private fun sourceFor(sourceId: String): FictionSource =
→ 38	        sources[sourceId] ?: error("Unknown source: $sourceId")
→ 39	}
→ 40	```
→ 41	
→ 42	Each source module contributes via Hilt `@IntoMap @StringKey`:
→ 43	
→ 44	```kotlin
→ 45	@Binds @IntoMap @StringKey("royalroad")
→ 46	abstract fun bindRoyalRoad(impl: RoyalRoadSource): FictionSource
→ 47	
→ 48	@Binds @IntoMap @StringKey("github")
→ 49	abstract fun bindGitHub(impl: GitHubSource): FictionSource
→ 50	```
→ 51	
→ 52	Routing key is the `sourceId` already stored on `FictionSummary` and persisted in Room.
→ 53	
→ 54	### `:source-github` module shape
→ 55	
→ 56	Mirrors `:source-royalroad`:
→ 57	
→ 58	```
→ 59	source-github/
→ 60	  src/main/kotlin/in/jphe/storyvox/source/github/
→ 61	    GitHubSource.kt              ← FictionSource impl
→ 62	    GitHubApi.kt                 ← OkHttp + kotlinx.serialization client
→ 63	    manifest/
→ 64	      BookManifest.kt            ← parsed book.toml + storyvox.json union
→ 65	      ManifestParser.kt
→ 66	    parser/
→ 67	      MarkdownChapterRenderer.kt ← MD → HTML (commonmark) + plaintext
→ 68	    registry/
→ 69	      Registry.kt                ← reads & caches storyvox-registry JSON
→ 70	    di/
→ 71	      GitHubModule.kt
→ 72	```
→ 73	
→ 74	## Manifest convention
→ 75	
→ 76	Two formats accepted, in priority order:
→ 77	
→ 78	### 1. `book.toml` (mdbook standard) — primary
→ 79	
→ 80	```toml
→ 81	[book]
→ 82	title = "The Archmage Coefficient"
→ 83	authors = ["onedayokay"]
→ 84	description = "An overpowered archmage..."
→ 85	language = "en"
→ 86	src = "src"
→ 87	```
→ 88	
→ 89	mdbook also implies: `src/SUMMARY.md` lists chapters in order, e.g.:
→ 90	
→ 91	```markdown
→ 92	# Summary
→ 93	- [Master Elric and the Spirit Orbs](chapters/01-master-elric.md)
→ 94	- [The Brass Gate](chapters/02-brass-gate.md)
→ 95	```
→ 96	
→ 97	This gives us title, author, chapter order, chapter file paths — enough for the listing.
→ 98	
→ 99	### 2. `storyvox.json` — extension manifest (optional)
→ 100	
→ 101	For metadata mdbook doesn't have:
→ 102	
→ 103	```json
→ 104	{
→ 105	  "version": 1,
→ 106	  "cover": "assets/cover.png",
→ 107	  "tags": ["fantasy", "litrpg"],
→ 108	  "status": "ongoing",
→ 109	  "narrator_voice_id": "en-US-Andrew:DragonHDLatestNeural",
→ 110	  "honeypot_selectors": []
→ 111	}
→ 112	```
→ 113	
→ 114	Lives at the repo root; entirely optional. If absent, defaults: no cover, empty tags, status=ONGOING, no voice override.
→ 115	
→ 116	### 3. Bare-repo fallback
→ 117	
→ 118	If neither manifest exists but the repo has a `chapters/` or `src/` directory containing numbered markdown files matching `^\d+[-_].*\.md$`, treat it as a fiction with:
→ 119	
→ 120	- `title` = repo name (kebab-case → Title Case)
[Read /home/jp/Projects/storyvox-worktrees/yara-mempalace-integration-spec/docs/superpowers/specs/2026-05-06-github-source-design.md:120-290]
→ 120	- `title` = repo name (kebab-case → Title Case)
→ 121	- `author` = repo owner login
→ 122	- `chapters` = sorted by leading number, title from `# Heading` of each file
→ 123	
→ 124	Lets users point at unmarked-up repos without forcing them to add a manifest, while documenting the manifest as the canonical path.
→ 125	
→ 126	## Chapter rendering
→ 127	
→ 128	- Markdown → HTML via `org.commonmark:commonmark` (small, no jvm-only deps).
→ 129	- HTML → plaintext via storyvox's existing `HoneypotFilter` pipeline plus a markdown-aware stripper.
→ 130	- Honor `storyvox.json.honeypot_selectors` if present (allows authors to mark "audio-only" or "visual-only" passages).
→ 131	
→ 132	## Identifiers
→ 133	
→ 134	A GitHub fiction's stable id is `github:owner/repo` (lowercased). Examples:
→ 135	
→ 136	```
→ 137	github:jphein/example-fiction
→ 138	github:onedayokay/the-archmage-coefficient
→ 139	```
→ 140	
→ 141	Chapter ids are `<fictionId>:<chapter-path-relative-to-repo>`:
→ 142	
→ 143	```
→ 144	github:jphein/example-fiction:src/01-elric.md
→ 145	```
→ 146	
→ 147	`sourceId` field stays `"github"`. Persisted in Room exactly the same as RR rows; the routing key is what's already there.
→ 148	
→ 149	## Discovery
→ 150	
→ 151	### Add by URL (primary entry point — accepts any source)
→ 152	
→ 153	Library tab gets a brass `+` FAB. The paste sheet is **source-agnostic** — paste anything, storyvox routes by URL pattern:
→ 154	
→ 155	| Pattern matched | Routed to |
→ 156	|---|---|
→ 157	| `https://www.royalroad.com/fiction/{id}` (and `/chapter/{id}`) | `:source-royalroad` |
→ 158	| `https://github.com/{owner}/{repo}` (and `/tree/{branch}`) | `:source-github` |
→ 159	| `github:{owner}/{repo}` or `{owner}/{repo}` | `:source-github` |
→ 160	
→ 161	A small `UrlRouter` in `:core-data` runs the regex match and returns `Pair<sourceId, fictionId>`; the repo's `addByUrl(url)` looks the source up via the new multi-binding map and calls `fictionDetail(fictionId)` on it. Same code path the registry uses internally.
→ 162	
→ 163	Resolver flow per source:
→ 164	
→ 165	- **Royal Road**: existing detail-page fetch path; nothing new.
→ 166	- **GitHub**: `GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}` → existence check → manifest fetch → parse → seed `FictionDetail` and persist.
→ 167	
→ 168	Either way the fiction shows up in Library immediately and the user lands on its detail screen.
→ 169	
→ 170	### Registry (curated, featured row)
→ 171	
→ 172	A separate `jphein/storyvox-registry` repo holds `registry.json`:
→ 173	
→ 174	```json
→ 175	{
→ 176	  "version": 1,
→ 177	  "fictions": [
→ 178	    {
→ 179	      "id": "github:jphein/example-fiction",
→ 180	      "tags": ["fantasy", "demo"],
→ 181	      "featured": false,
→ 182	      "added_at": "2026-05-06"
→ 183	    }
→ 184	  ]
→ 185	}
→ 186	```
→ 187	
→ 188	storyvox fetches `registry.json` from `raw.githubusercontent.com` once per session (cached). Registry entries appear as the **Featured** row at the top of Browse → GitHub — pinned, curated, hand-picked. The rest of the screen is Search.
→ 189	
→ 190	### Browse + filter (Search-driven)
→ 191	
→ 192	Browse → GitHub is a real searchable surface, not just the registry. Two queries run in parallel:
→ 193	
→ 194	1. **`GET /search/repositories?q=topic:fiction+{userQuery}`** — pulls anything on GitHub tagged `topic:fiction` (or `topic:fanfiction`, `topic:webnovel` — registry-configurable union of topics). 30 req/min unauthenticated, plenty for a typed-search UX with a 300ms debounce.
→ 195	2. **Registry filter** — same `userQuery` filtered client-side over the cached registry list.
→ 196	
→ 197	Results merge with registry entries pinned to the top.
→ 198	
→ 199	#### Filter dimensions
→ 200	
→ 201	The filter sheet (mirrors the existing Royal Road BrowseFilter) exposes:
→ 202	
→ 203	| Filter | Source of truth | Behaviour when missing |
→ 204	|---|---|---|
→ 205	| **Tags** (multi-select: fantasy, litrpg, fanfic, sci-fi, …) | `storyvox.json.tags`, else GitHub `topics` array | Empty tag list = unfiltered |
→ 206	| **Status** (ongoing / completed / hiatus / dropped) | `storyvox.json.status`, else inferred from days-since-last-commit (>180d=hiatus, >365d=dropped, default=ongoing) | Default to ongoing |
→ 207	| **Length** (min / max chapter count) | `SUMMARY.md` parsed length, cached | Treated as "unknown", excluded from min-chapter filters but included from max |
→ 208	| **Last updated** (cutoff) | repo's `pushed_at` from API | Always available |
→ 209	| **Min stars** | repo `stargazers_count` | Always available |
→ 210	| **Language** | repo `language` (ISO code in `book.toml.language` overrides) | Defaults to `en` |
→ 211	| **Sort** | popularity (stars), recency (`pushed_at`), title | Always available |
→ 212	
→ 213	**Filter quality scales with manifest adoption.** Repos with a `book.toml` + `storyvox.json` filter precisely; bare repos still filter on what GitHub itself exposes (stars, recency, language, topics). The "Add manifest to your repo" doc on the storyvox README is what we point authors at.
→ 214	
→ 215	#### Caching
→ 216	
→ 217	- **Search results** — keyed by `(query, filters)` → cached 30 minutes in Room. Stale-while-revalidate so scrolling the same filter is instant.
→ 218	- **Repo metadata** — keyed by `github:owner/repo` → cached until the repo's `pushed_at` changes. The cache row also stores parsed manifest blob.
→ 219	- **Registry JSON** — fetched once per session, cached in memory.
→ 220	
→ 221	The 60-req/hr unauthenticated cap is fine: typical browse session is one search + one or two filter changes ≈ 4 search calls + 0–10 metadata fetches for visible cards. Aggressive caching keeps a power user under the cap.
→ 222	
→ 223	## Auth + rate limits
→ 224	
→ 225	- v1 ships **unauthenticated** — 60 GitHub API requests/hour per IP.
→ 226	- Manifest + chapter reads use `raw.githubusercontent.com` which is **not rate-limited** the same way.
→ 227	- Add an optional **GitHub PAT** field in Settings → "Sources" for power users. PAT lifts the limit to 5,000/hr. Stored in `EncryptedSharedPreferences` next to RR cookies.
→ 228	- 60/hr is enough for everyday use as long as we cache aggressively and only hit `/repos/...` on the initial add and on explicit refresh.
→ 229	
→ 230	## Sync model
→ 231	
→ 232	- "New chapter" detection: store the latest commit SHA we've seen for each fiction. On refresh, `GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/commits?since={timestamp}` → if any commit changed a chapter file, mark fiction "has updates" and re-fetch SUMMARY.md.
→ 233	- Existing `ChapterDownloadWorker` reused — just hits a different URL.
→ 234	- `WorkManager` poll interval respects user's existing setting from Settings → Downloads → "Poll every Nh".
→ 235	
→ 236	## Honeypot / TTS hygiene
→ 237	
→ 238	GitHub markdown is much cleaner than RR's HTML — no `display:none; speak:never` anti-piracy spans. Default honeypot config is empty. Authors who want audio-only or visual-only blocks declare them in `storyvox.json.honeypot_selectors`.
→ 239	
→ 240	## UI surface changes
→ 241	
→ 242	| Surface | Change |
→ 243	|--------|--------|
→ 244	| Library | Add brass `+` FAB → "Add fiction by URL" sheet |
→ 245	| Browse | Top-level tabs become `Royal Road / GitHub` once GitHub source is enabled |
→ 246	| Settings | New "Sources" section with GitHub PAT input + registry-refresh button |
→ 247	| Fiction detail | New chip showing source: `📚 Royal Road` or `🐙 GitHub` |
→ 248	| Reader | No change |
→ 249	
→ 250	## Build sequence
→ 251	
→ 252	Implementation order so each step ships value on its own:
→ 253	
→ 254	1. **Multi-source refactor** — Hilt multibindings, `sourceFor(id)` in repos. Keep one source bound. No user-visible change. *Tag: storyvox v0.3.x*
→ 255	2. **`UrlRouter` + paste-anything sheet** — works against current single source (RR). Library `+` FAB, regex-routed. *Visible win even before GitHub source exists.*
→ 256	3. **`:source-github` module + `GitHubApi`** — OkHttp client, models for `repo`, `commit`, `tree`, `search`. Just talks to API. No UI.
→ 257	4. **Manifest parsing** — `BookManifest` reading `book.toml` + `storyvox.json` + bare-repo fallback. Unit-tested with example repos.
→ 258	5. **Chapter rendering** — markdown → HTML/plaintext path. Reader works with a manually-seeded GitHub fiction.
→ 259	6. **Add-by-URL routes to GitHub** — paste sheet now resolves GitHub URLs end-to-end through `:source-github`.
→ 260	7. **Browse → GitHub: registry-only** — Featured row from `registry.json`. Lands the curated surface without depending on Search yet.
→ 261	8. **Browse → GitHub: search + filters** — wire `/search/repositories`, build the filter sheet, merge results with registry.
→ 262	9. **Sync** — commit-SHA-based polling. ChapterDownloadWorker hits raw.githubusercontent.com.
→ 263	
→ 264	Each step ships value on its own. Steps 1–2 are pure refactor + UX win; step 7 lands the curated browse surface; step 8 is the full filter UX.
→ 265	
→ 266	Each step is its own commit + tag candidate. Step 1 alone is meaningful (cleans up an architectural assumption); step 5 is the first user-visible win.
→ 267	
→ 268	## Tradeoffs called out
→ 269	
→ 270	- **Browse divergence** — RR's "popular" / "latest" / "best by genre" don't translate to GitHub. The Browse tab grows a source picker; UI patterns diverge by source. Acceptable, but means "Browse" is no longer a single concept.
→ 271	- **Manifest discovery vs heuristic** — preferring `book.toml` is friendly to the existing mdbook ecosystem but adds a parse path. Bare-repo fallback prevents that from being a hard requirement at the cost of guessing structure.
→ 272	- **No GitHub Search** — discovery is limited to "users paste URLs" + curated registry. Could extend later but Search API quality is poor for fiction.
→ 273	- **PAT in encrypted prefs** — same security model storyvox already uses for RR cookies; not new attack surface but worth being deliberate about.
→ 274	- **GPL-3.0 implications** — storyvox is GPL-3.0 (matches VoxSherpa); the registry repo can be a mix of MIT/CC0 metadata referencing fictions under whatever license. We don't redistribute fiction text — we link to it — so compatibility is the fiction author's problem.
→ 275	
→ 276	## Decisions (so far)
→ 277	
→ 278	- **2026-05-06 — Add-by-URL accepts any source.** Paste sheet is source-agnostic; URL pattern decides routing.
→ 279	- **2026-05-06 — Registry: JP-owned, no PR governance.** `jphein/storyvox-registry` with informal direct commits; revisit if external contributors show up.
→ 280	- **2026-05-06 — GitHub PAT: not in v1.** Ship unauthenticated (60 req/hr). Add optional Settings → Sources → "GitHub token" once anyone hits the limit.
→ 281	- **2026-05-06 — GitHub Search API is in scope for v1.** Browse → GitHub combines a registry "Featured" row with `/search/repositories?q=topic:fiction+...` results, filtered by tags/status/length/recency/stars/language. Filter quality scales with manifest adoption; bare repos still filter on what GitHub itself exposes.
→ 282	
→ 283	## Open questions
→ 284	
→ 285	None blocking implementation. Spec is ready to execute.
→ 286
Excellent — I now have a clear template. I have everything I need. Let me write the spec, then the implementation.

[{"body":"https://www.csail.mit.edu/news/teaching-ai-models-say-im-not-sure","labels":[],"number":84,"title":"research and integrate"},{"body":"## Background

The 2026-05-15 chunking×encoder reproduction (see [#1384](https://github.com/MemPalace/mempalace/discussions/1384) thread + local artifact at \`scripts/verify_rrf_3way.py\`) measured **+0.0841 MRR vs best solo** when fusing default ONNX MiniLM with two adaptmem FT-Code SentenceTransformer checkpoints under 3-way RRF on the n=200 git-derived probe set. Reproduced @nakata-app's #1384 §4 inversion at 10× their sample size:

| Encoder | Solo MRR | Recall@10 |
|---|---:|---:|
| default ONNX | 0.4260 | 49.5% (99/200) |
| FT-Code-1000 | 0.4229 | 53.5% (107/200) |
| FT-Code-5000 | 0.3972 | 50.0% (100/200) |
| **RRF 3-way** | **0.5101** | **59.5% (119/200)** |

This is a real, statistically-grounded retrieval-quality lever that our current `candidate_strategy="hybrid"` (vector ∪ BM25 ∪ graph) does not exploit — we run **one** encoder on the vector axis.

## Why this issue exists (and why it's not a PR yet)

The lift is real, but the engineering cost of productizing it is non-trivial:

- **~Nx embed compute on ingest** (currently single-encoder ONNX MiniLM CPU; 3x means either 3 sequential calls per drawer or a multi-encoder batched path).
- **~Nx vector storage** per drawer (postgres backend → either N vector columns on `mempalace_drawers`, or N separate tables with the same primary key, or an `embedding_kind` discriminator on a single column).
- **~Nx vector-search latency at query time** (parallelizable across N pgvector indexes; latency floor is the slowest encoder).
- **N model files to ship/manage** — `mempalace[gpu]` extra currently pins one ONNX runtime; multi-encoder means a `sentence-transformers` install or an N-model ONNX bundle.

And the lift was measured on **commit-subject-shaped probes** (queries derived from git log subjects), not user-style natural-language queries. Whether the lift translates is open. Our current hybrid retrieval may already be capturing most of the gap on real queries via the BM25 + graph paths.

## Gate: HyDE diagnosis first

HyDE (query-side rewriter that bridges vocabulary gaps via LLM-written hypothetical answers) is the other lever for the same problem class — vocabulary mismatch between query and drawer. Costs ~one LLM call (~2s on gemma3:4b, ~3s on qwen2.5:14b) per query but **adds nothing to ingest or storage**. The probe runner in `familiar.realm.watch` exists (see [familiar#5](https://github.com/techempower-org/familiar.realm.watch/issues/5)) but the first A/B run on katana+qwen2.5:14b showed **0 rescues / 0 regressions on 15 probes** ([familiar#6](https://github.com/techempower-org/familiar.realm.watch/issues/6) tracks diagnosis).

If HyDE proves out (a rerun on a properly-anchored probe set or with a stronger HyDE model shows real rescues), then HyDE is the cheap right answer and we don't need multi-encoder. If HyDE proves not to work for our corpus, then this issue becomes the next-best lever.

## Sketch of what the work would look like

If we end up implementing this:

1. **Backend abstraction**: extend `PostgresBackend.add_to_collection()` to accept an `embeddings_by_kind: dict[str, list[float]]` shape, with `kind` corresponding to a configured encoder identity. Default kind stays the only one for backwards compat. (~50 lines of `mempalace/backends/postgres.py`.)

2. **Schema**: add `mempalace_drawer_embeddings(drawer_id FK, kind TEXT, vec vector(384))` with HNSW indexes per `kind`. Existing `mempalace_drawers.embedding` stays as the default-kind index for backwards compat.

3. **Encoder config**: extend `~/.mempalace/config.yaml` with an `encoders:` list (default kind + optional additional kinds with model paths). `get_embedding_function()` returns a dict-of-functions.

4. **Ingest pipeline**: `miner.mine` / `silent_save` etc. compute embeddings for all configured encoders. Falls back gracefully if an extra-encoder model is missing.

5. **Retrieval**: extend `candidate_strategy` with a new value `"hybrid_multi_encoder"` (or an `encoders=[...]` parameter on `"hybrid"`) that fans out vector candidates per encoder, RRF-fuses with the existing BM25 + graph paths.

6. **Reranker**: existing `_hybrid_rank` should mostly work; may need to handle the multi-encoder cosine values (likely just average them, or use the best per drawer).

7. **Eval gate**: the new candidate strategy lands behind a benchmark gate — require `n>=200` git-derived probes showing measurable lift vs single-encoder hybrid before flipping the default.

## Open questions

1. Is there a smaller-N variant that captures most of the lift? Our data shows the **largest 2-way fusion lift comes from default + FT-Code-5000** (+0.0631), not from adding more encoders. Maybe 2-encoder is the right shape.
2. Does **distillation** (training a single encoder to mimic the ensemble's behavior on our corpus) capture the lift without the 2-3x runtime cost? @nakata-app's adaptmem methodology is the obvious tool here.
3. How does this interact with **chromadb backend** (which still works behind `MEMPALACE_BACKEND=chroma`)? Probably means multi-encoder is postgres-only — chromadb users stay on single-encoder.

## Acceptance / closing this issue

- [ ] HyDE diagnosis (familiar#6) lands a verdict
- [ ] If HyDE works → close this as "deferred — vocabulary bridge covered by HyDE"
- [ ] If HyDE doesn't work → write up a design RFC, gather feedback before implementation

Linked to [techempower-org/mempalace#77](https://github.com/techempower-org/mempalace/pull/77) (RRF verifier source) and [MemPalace/mempalace#1384](https://github.com/MemPalace/mempalace/discussions/1384) (the encoder-axis discussion that surfaced this).","labels":[],"number":82,"title":"Research: multi-encoder retrieval (RRF over N encoders) as the next lever after HyDE diagnosis lands"},{"body":"## The question

`kind=` was retired in [`7ba28dc`](https://github.com/techempower-org/mempalace/commit/7ba28dc) (2026-04-27) after the Phase A–E checkpoint-collection split made it inert: all Stop-hook checkpoints moved to `mempalace_session_recovery`, the main `mempalace_drawers` collection ended up with 0 entries tagged `topic=checkpoint` (763 of them in the recovery collection), and the `kind=content` filter was filtering nothing. The daemon dropped its `_VALID_KINDS` validation in lockstep (cf. palace-daemon `CHANGELOG.md`).

That fix was correct for what `kind=` was doing at the time. **But the underlying design need — "let consumers scope a search across a subset of palace storage" — didn't go away with `kind=`'s retirement; it just stopped being visible.**

Now that the postgres+pgvector+AGE migration is landing and the palace has at least two physically separate stores (`mempalace_drawers` for content, `mempalace_session_recovery` for checkpoints, potentially more as the architecture evolves), the design question deserves a fresh look:

**Should `mempalace_search` and `/search` expose a general scope/collection filter, or should multi-collection access stay implicit (search reads the main collection, recovery/audit tools read recovery)?**

## What the retirement *didn't* settle

`kind=` was a binary filter on a single collection. The new architecture has N collections, and the framing question is different from what `7ba28dc` was answering:

| Question 7ba28dc answered | Question the new architecture asks |
|---|---|
| Should `mempalace_search` filter out checkpoint drawers within `mempalace_drawers`? | Should `mempalace_search` ever read from *other* collections? |
| If yes, with a `kind=` parameter? | If yes, with what surface (parameter, separate tool, scope-set)? |
| → No, because the structural fix made the filter inert | → ??? |

The answer might still be "no, separate concerns belong to separate tools." It also might be "yes, with `mempalace_search(scope=['drawers','session_recovery'])` or similar." Either is defensible; the work to do is the comparison, not the implementation.

## Specific design alternatives worth weighing

1. **Status quo, document it.** `mempalace_search` reads `mempalace_drawers` only. Recovery/audit lives in `mempalace_recover_session` and friends. Cross-collection search isn't supported and isn't planned. Doc it in the README + each MCP tool's description so consumers stop asking. Cheapest, ratifies what's already true.

2. **Scope parameter on `mempalace_search`.** Add a `collections: list[str] | None = None` parameter (default `["mempalace_drawers"]`, accepts an enum of known collection names). Generalises the deprecated binary `kind=`. Symmetric with the postgres+pgvector model where collections are first-class. Cost: every collection registered to the search path needs its embedding/ranking to be comparable; mixing trigram-only and pgvector collections in one ranked result is non-trivial.

3. **Separate per-collection search tools.** `mempalace_search` stays drawers-only. Add `mempalace_search_recovery` for session-recovery audit, future `mempalace_search_<X>` per collection. Most additive, lets each collection have its own ranking semantics. Cost: consumers who genuinely need cross-collection results assemble them client-side.

4. **Federated search with a single tool.** `mempalace_search` reads all registered collections by default, with rankers fused. Most powerful, highest implementation cost (need a fusion strategy that doesn't degenerate when collections have different scales or distributions).

I have no strong opinion on which is right — this is the kind of design call where downstream consumers' actual needs should weigh more than my theoretical preference.

## What I'm hitting downstream

The reason this came up today: SME's [`MemPalaceDaemonAdapter`](https://github.com/M0nkeyFl0wer/multipass-structural-memory-eval/blob/main/sme/adapters/mempalace_daemon.py) still carries `DEFAULT_KIND = "content"` and a `--kind` CLI flag from when `kind=` was a real parameter. The adapter sends `kind=content` on every `/search` call, which the daemon now silently ignores. Live test `test_kind_content_excludes_stop_hook_checkpoints` fails on machines where the daemon is reachable — not because the daemon is broken, but because the test is asserting on a retired feature. I'm xfailing that test as part of the SME PR #7 cleanup with a pointer back to this issue.

If the answer here is **Option 1 (status quo, document)**, the SME-side cleanup is just: remove `DEFAULT_KIND`, remove the `--kind` CLI flag, delete the xfail'd test entirely, ship as part of the next SME release. That's straightforward.

If the answer is **Option 2 or 4 (parameter on search)**, SME's `--kind` becomes the bones of a richer `--scope` flag — and the test gets resurrected with new asserts. The current xfail becomes a TODO comment instead of a deletion.

If the answer is **Option 3 (separate tools)**, the SME adapter grows a sibling for any collection it wants to introspect — and the `--kind` CLI flag becomes an `--adapter mempalace-daemon-recovery` choice instead.

## No urgency, but it'd be nice to know

This isn't blocking anything I'm working on — the xfail clears the suite for PR #7 merge. But it'd be worth resolving before the next SME release so the vestigial `--kind` machinery doesn't outlive the feature it was wrapping by months.

Also flagging this is *probably* my last open SME↔mempalace boundary question for the moment — the `list_tunnels` divergence (#75) and this one were both surfaced in the same diagnostic pass against the live palace. No other latent inconsistencies caught yet.
","labels":[],"number":76,"title":"Design call: bring back a scope/collection filter on search now that the palace has multiple stores?"},{"body":"## Summary

The `mempalace_list_tunnels` MCP tool surfaces only **explicit** tunnels (agent-created, persisted at `~/.mempalace/tunnels.json`). It has no way to expose **passive** tunnels — the rooms-appearing-in-≥2-wings inference that `graph_stats.top_tunnels` already computes. Consumers calling `list_tunnels` on a palace with passive structure but no explicit tunnels get `[]` and conclude "no tunnels," which is misleading.

palace-daemon's `GET /graph` endpoint already works around this client-side (cf. `palace-daemon/main.py:1660-1663` workaround comment: *"tunnels via top_tunnels (mempalace 3.3.4's mempalace_list_tunnels returns [] on palaces where graph_stats reports tunnels — bug tracked in docs/graph-endpoint.md Part 2)"*) by skipping `list_tunnels` and reaching into `graph_stats.top_tunnels` instead. Anyone calling the MCP surface directly — SME's `MemPalaceDaemonAdapter` MCP fallback, custom Claude Code agents, future integrations — hits the same gap.

## Diagnostic evidence (live, 2026-05-14)

Probed `disks.jphe.in:8085` running mempalace fork on the new postgres+pgvector+AGE backend (daemon 1.7.2):

```bash
$ curl -sS -H "X-API-Key: ***" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
    -X POST $PALACE_DAEMON_URL/mcp \
    -d '{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":1,"method":"tools/call",
         "params":{"name":"mempalace_list_tunnels","arguments":{}}}'
# → returns 0 items

$ curl -sS -H "X-API-Key: ***" $PALACE_DAEMON_URL/graph | jq '.tunnels | length'
# → 7
```

Same underlying palace, two MCP-accessible surfaces, two different counts. `/graph` is correct (7 passive tunnels exist in the structure); `list_tunnels` is correct *if* the consumer knows it only means explicit tunnels — but the MCP tool description doesn't disambiguate.

## Architecture context

From `mempalace/palace_graph.py` itself:

- L290-310: `graph_stats(col, config)` computes `top_tunnels` from `build_graph()` — the postgres+AGE-aware path that knows about cross-wing room memberships.
- L330-336 comment: *"Passive tunnels are discovered from shared room names across wings. Explicit tunnels are created by agents when they notice a connection between two specific drawers or rooms in different wings/projects. Stored as a JSON file at `~/.mempalace/tunnels.json` so they persist across palace rebuilds (not in ChromaDB which can be recreated)."*
- L503-517: `list_tunnels(wing=None)` reads only `_TUNNEL_FILE` — explicit only.

So the two kinds are intentional. The gap is that **the MCP surface only exposes one of them through `list_tunnels`**, and the other is buried inside `graph_stats` as a sub-field rather than being its own tool. That asymmetry is the actual bug.

## Proposed fix (non-breaking)

Option A — **add a parameter to `list_tunnels`**. Cleanest API symmetry:

```python
# mempalace/palace_graph.py
def list_tunnels(wing: str = None, include_passive: bool = False, config=None, col=None):
    explicit = _load_tunnels()
    if wing:
        norm = _normalize_wing(wing)
        explicit = [t for t in explicit
                    if t["source"]["wing"] == norm or t["target"]["wing"] == norm]
    if not include_passive:
        return explicit
    passive = graph_stats(col=col, config=config).get("top_tunnels", [])
    if wing:
        norm = _normalize_wing(wing)
        passive = [t for t in passive if norm in t.get("wings", [])]
    # Tag each with kind so consumers can tell them apart
    return ([{**t, "kind": "explicit"} for t in explicit]
            + [{**t, "kind": "passive"} for t in passive])
```

And the MCP tool description in `mcp_server.py` mentions both kinds:

```python
"mempalace_list_tunnels": {
    "description": "List cross-wing tunnels. Default returns only explicit "
                   "(agent-created) tunnels stored at ~/.mempalace/tunnels.json. "
                   "Pass include_passive=true to also include passive tunnels "
                   "(rooms appearing in 2+ wings, computed from graph_stats). "
                   "Each result is tagged with kind: 'explicit'|'passive'.",
    "handler": tool_list_tunnels,
    # ...
}
```

Option B — **add a separate `mempalace_list_passive_tunnels` tool**. Most additive, simplest:

```python
def tool_list_passive_tunnels(wing: str = None):
    """List passive cross-wing tunnels (rooms appearing in 2+ wings)."""
    passive = graph_stats().get("top_tunnels", [])
    if wing:
        norm = _normalize_wing(wing)
        passive = [t for t in passive if norm in t.get("wings", [])]
    return passive
```

I prefer Option A because the merged result with `kind:` tagging answers the consumer's intuitive question ("what tunnels does this palace have?") in one call, but either fix removes the palace-daemon workaround need.

## Downstream impact

After the fix:
- palace-daemon's `/graph` can drop the `graph_stats.top_tunnels` workaround and call `mempalace_list_tunnels(include_passive=True)` directly. Removes a coordination point between the two repos.
- SME's `MemPalaceDaemonAdapter` MCP fallback path becomes equivalent to the `/graph` path for tunnel discovery. Currently the fallback under-reports tunnels — Cat 5 / Cat 4 readings against pre-1.6.0 daemons would have been wrong.
- Future MCP consumers see both tunnel kinds with one call.

## Acceptance check

Live diagnostic that should pass after the fix:

```bash
# Should return 7 (matching /graph.tunnels)
curl -sS -H "X-API-Key: ***" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -X POST $PALACE_DAEMON_URL/mcp \
  -d '{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":1,"method":"tools/call",
       "params":{"name":"mempalace_list_tunnels",
                 "arguments":{"include_passive":true}}}' | \
  jq '.result.content[0].text | fromjson | length'
```

Happy to take this on if it's a wanted direction — small enough to land as a single PR with a regression test against a 2-wing-shared-room fixture.
","labels":[],"number":75,"title":"list_tunnels MCP tool surfaces only explicit tunnels; passive (room-shared-across-wings) tunnels invisible"},{"body":"## Symptom

Today on `disks`, `palace-daemon /mcp` wedged for 28+ minutes. Every concurrent Stop/PreCompact hook from active claude-code sessions queued at urlopen 30s and timed out. Root cause was three concurrent `CREATE INDEX "mempalace_drawers_vec_idx" USING hnsw` queries holding `ACCESS EXCLUSIVE` on `mempalace_drawers`, with a backlog of `INSERT`/`DELETE`/secondary-`CREATE INDEX` queued behind them.

## Root cause

`mempalace/backends/postgres.py:683` in `_maybe_create_vector_index`:

```python
index_name = f"{self.table_name}_vec_idx"
cur.execute("SELECT 1 FROM pg_indexes WHERE indexname = %s", (index_name,))
if cur.fetchone():
    self._vector_index_ready = True
    return
# ... falls through to CREATE INDEX
cur.execute(
    self._sql.SQL("CREATE INDEX {} ON {} USING {} (embedding {})").format(...)
)
```

Two compounding bugs:

1. **Name-coupled existence check.** The check is by literal name `{table_name}_vec_idx`. In our deployment the HNSW index was created out-of-band by the migration tool under the name `mempalace_drawers_embedding_hnsw`. Functionally a valid HNSW index on the same column, but `_maybe_create_vector_index` doesn't see it — falls through to CREATE INDEX every time the row threshold trips.

2. **Lost-update race between connections.** No advisory lock, no `IF NOT EXISTS`, no inter-process coordination. Under `PALACE_MAX_CONCURRENCY=4` with multiple concurrent writers crossing the threshold simultaneously (e.g. 3 Stop hooks firing in the same second), each connection does the same SELECT/CREATE pair. All three SELECTs return empty; all three then issue `CREATE INDEX` and grab `ACCESS EXCLUSIVE`. They serialize, but they all run and they all block writes for the duration.

The second bug is the dangerous one — even without the name mismatch, three concurrent writers tipping the threshold within the same write batch can each race here and stack three identical full HNSW builds.

## Reproduction

1. Have ≥`VECTOR_INDEX_MIN_ROWS` (≈5k) drawers, no HNSW yet under the expected name.
2. Three concurrent writers, each inserting enough rows to bump `_rows_since_index_check` past `VECTOR_INDEX_CHECK_INTERVAL_ROWS`.
3. All three call `_maybe_create_vector_index`, all three SELECT empty, all three CREATE INDEX.

In our case this manifested as `palace-daemon /mcp` wedging — caller-visible 30s+ urlopen timeouts on every Stop hook for a sustained period.

## Mitigation applied locally

Out-of-band: cancelled the duplicate `CREATE INDEX` backends (`pg_cancel_backend`), let one complete, dropped the legacy-named `mempalace_drawers_embedding_hnsw` duplicate (308 MB → reclaimed), preserved the new `mempalace_drawers_vec_idx` (371 MB, valid). Daemon `/mcp` round-trip dropped from 30 s timeout to 12 ms.

## Proposed fix

A few independent improvements, in order of priority:

1. **Existence check should be by structure, not name.** Query `pg_index` for any valid HNSW index on `(embedding vector_cosine_ops)` against this table, regardless of name. Catches operator-installed indexes and migration-tool-created ones.
2. **Wrap the lookup + create in a session advisory lock** keyed on the table name. Only one connection per database at a time goes through the create path.
3. **Use `CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS`** as a belt-and-suspenders against the lookup-vs-create race.
4. **(Optional) Consider `CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY`** so the index build doesn't take `ACCESS EXCLUSIVE` and stall every other writer. The `CONCURRENTLY` variant is slower (≈ 2×) but won't wedge concurrent traffic. pgvector ≥ 0.5.0 supports it for `hnsw`.

I can put up a fork PR if useful — wanted to file the diagnosis first.

## Context

- Fork: `techempower-org/mempalace`
- Inherits from upstream cherry-pick of [MemPalace/mempalace#665](https://github.com/MemPalace/mempalace/pull/665) (PR #21 here)
- Triggered by: `palace-daemon` calling pgvector backend under `PALACE_MAX_CONCURRENCY=4`
- pgvector version: 0.8.2
- Postgres 16 (apache/age image) on disks
- Drawer count when wedge hit: 271,348","labels":[{"id":"LA_kwDOR-Npq88AAAACetPgJA","name":"bug","description":"Something isn't working","color":"d73a4a"}],"number":73,"title":"PostgresBackend._maybe_create_vector_index: race + name-mismatch wedges database under concurrent writes"},{"body":"## Why this exists

Today's pgvector migration dry-run (Phase 4.1 of the migration plan) blocked on this: `mempalace/migrate_to_postgres.py::phase_2_drawers` opens the source palace via `chromadb.PersistentClient(path=...)`, which SIGSEGVs on long-lived palaces. See chroma-core/chroma#6949 — even `col.get(include=["metadatas"])` crashes the Rust binding on this palace.

The aborted dry-run was 5+ hours into a `mempalace repair --mode from-sqlite` that was just doing what the migration tool itself should be doing (reading raw sqlite + re-embedding). I aborted it (#65 earlier today) and built a separate pipeline:

1. `extract_drawers.py` — reads `chroma.sqlite3` via raw sqlite, dumps `drawers.jsonl`
2. `embed.py` — re-embeds on GPU via sentence-transformers (6 min for 271k drawers on a 2080 Ti)
3. `load_pgvector.py` — bulk INSERT to pgvector with HNSW + GIN indexes

Total migration: ~14 min wall-clock vs the aborted ~7h repair. The trick wasn't optimization, it was **bypassing chromadb's Python API entirely**.

## What needs to change in the migration tool

`migrate_to_postgres.phase_2_drawers` should:

- [ ] **Default**: read raw sqlite via `sqlite3` (stdlib), pivot `embedding_metadata` EAV → per-drawer dicts. Pattern documented in `extract_drawers.py` from the working pipeline.
- [ ] **Embedding**: don't recompute. The chromadb HNSW segment binary files have them, but they're the corruption locus; raw sqlite has `embeddings.embedding_id` pointers but the actual vectors live in the HNSW binaries (lost on corruption). So this phase MUST re-embed via the configured embedding function. Document the trade-off.
- [ ] **GPU friendly**: support `--embed-on-host=URL-to-daemon` so a slow source host (e.g. disks) can do the read while a fast embed host (e.g. katana w/ GPU) does the vectorization. Today's pipeline cheats by doing both on katana.
- [ ] **Fallback**: keep the current chromadb-open path as a `--source=chromadb-direct` option for palaces that aren't broken. Default switches to `--source=sqlite-raw`.

## Adjacent fork-roadmap items

- The migration tool's `phase_5_kg` (sqlite → AGE) uses `KnowledgeGraphAGE.add_triple()`, which had the AGE-parameter-binding bug fixed today in 74ddf6f. Worth verifying phase_5_kg integration tests against a real Postgres+AGE before merging the rewrite above.
- The `load_pgvector.py` script we built today should fold into the migration tool as the postgres-write side once the raw-sqlite read side is in.

## Files involved

- `mempalace/migrate_to_postgres.py` — `phase_2_drawers` (most of the work)
- `tests/test_migrate_to_postgres.py` — phase_2 tests rewrite
- The standalone `extract_drawers.py` and `load_pgvector.py` from today's session at `disks:/mnt/raid/projects/mempalace-data/palace-snapshot-2026-05-13.tar.gz` are the reference implementation.","labels":[{"id":"LA_kwDOR-Npq88AAAACetPgJA","name":"bug","description":"Something isn't working","color":"d73a4a"},{"id":"LA_kwDOR-Npq88AAAACetPgRw","name":"enhancement","description":"New feature or request","color":"a2eeef"}],"number":70,"title":"Fork-roadmap: rewrite migrate_to_postgres phase_2_drawers to read raw sqlite, not chromadb"},{"body":"## Why this exists

Upstream [MemPalace/mempalace#1069](https://github.com/MemPalace/mempalace/issues/1069) wants to consolidate the per-event `.sh` hook wrappers (`mempal_save_hook.sh`, `mempal_precompact_hook.sh`, etc.) into thin shims that delegate to **`mempalace hook run --hook <name>`** — i.e., move all hook logic into the `mempalace` Python CLI.

This fork went the **opposite direction**: the `.sh` shims delegate to **`palace-daemon/clients/hook.py`** (a stdlib-only Python script in a different repo). The `mempalace` Python package is no longer in the hook call path at all.

## Trade-offs we chose

| | Upstream #1069 direction | This fork's direction |
|---|---|---|
| Single entry point | `mempalace hook run` (Python CLI) | `palace-daemon/clients/hook.py` (stdlib Python) |
| `mempalace` import dep | Required at hook time | Not used by the hook |
| Daemon-routed mining | Optional via `PALACE_DAEMON_URL` | Always-on, via HTTP |
| Write authority | Library-level lock in `mempalace` | Single-writer gateway (daemon `_mine_sem`) |
| CLI version-mismatch hangs | Vulnerable (see upstream #1465) | Sidestepped — no CLI in the call path |
| Failure mode if daemon down | n/a | Hook returns `{}` (silent), no error |

## Where the divergence lives in this fork

- `.claude-plugin/hooks/mempal-stop-hook.sh` — calls `palace-daemon/clients/hook.py --hook stop --harness claude-code`
- `.claude-plugin/hooks/mempal-precompact-hook.sh` — same shape, `precompact` event
- `.claude-plugin/hooks/hooks.json` — registers `SessionStart` directly to the daemon's hook.py (no `.sh` shim)
- `.codex-plugin/hooks/mempal-hook.sh` — same pattern for the codex plugin
- `palace-daemon/clients/hook.py` lives at https://github.com/jphein/palace-daemon/blob/main/clients/hook.py

## Roadmap items implied by this divergence

- [ ] If we ever re-converge with upstream's #1069, the shims here need swapping back to `mempalace hook run`. Decision point: re-evaluate whenever upstream's gateway recommendation lands (the active discussion is at MemPalace/mempalace#1497).
- [ ] Document this divergence in the project CLAUDE.md so a future agent doesn't "fix" the shims to match upstream.
- [ ] Consider whether the `.sh` shims are even needed long-term: `hooks.json` can call `python3 /path/to/hook.py` directly without the bash intermediary. The shim only exists for backward-compat with old Claude Code sessions that loaded the pre-2026-05-11 hook config.

## Related

- Upstream issue this responds to: MemPalace/mempalace#1069
- The gateway recommendation discussion: MemPalace/mempalace#1497 (this fork is referenced there as a working example)
- The split-brain fix that landed our current direction: this fork's commit (2026-05-11 eaf0e2c → onward)","labels":[{"id":"LA_kwDOR-Npq88AAAACetPgMg","name":"documentation","description":"Improvements or additions to documentation","color":"0075ca"},{"id":"LA_kwDOR-Npq88AAAACetPgRw","name":"enhancement","description":"New feature or request","color":"a2eeef"}],"number":69,"title":"Fork-roadmap: keep .sh shims delegating to palace-daemon (counter-direction to upstream #1069)"},{"body":"## Why this exists

`docs/fork-changes.yaml` is the canonical source for fork-ahead-change history; `FORK_CHANGELOG.md` is rendered from it via `scripts/render-docs.py`. The workflow in `CLAUDE.md` says:

> 1. Land the code change with a focused commit on `main`.
> 2. Add an entry to `docs/fork-changes.yaml`.
> 3. Run `scripts/render-docs.py` to regenerate `FORK_CHANGELOG.md`.
> 4. Run `scripts/check-docs.sh` to verify nothing has drifted.
> 5. Commit the YAML + the regenerated `FORK_CHANGELOG.md` together.

Steps 3 and 4 are **manual** today. `scripts/check-docs.sh` (check 3) catches the drift when run, but nothing forces it to run.

## What to do

Auto-trigger `render-docs.py` (or at minimum the `--check` mode) on every commit that touches `docs/fork-changes.yaml`. Two paths:

**Path A — local pre-commit hook.** Add `.githooks/pre-commit` (or wire into `pre-commit` if/when the fork adopts it) that runs `scripts/render-docs.py --check` if `docs/fork-changes.yaml` is staged. Fast-fail with a one-line message and the regenerate command.

**Path B — GitHub Action.** Add `.github/workflows/check-docs.yml` running `scripts/check-docs.sh --quiet` on PRs that touch `docs/`. Catches PRs that bypass local hooks. CI cost is trivial — the script is ~100 lines of bash + a python --check call.

**Recommendation**: Path B first (catches all paths including human PRs that don't go through this fork's local tooling). Path A as a nice-to-have. They compose; both can land.

## Same workflow problem, related symptom

`scripts/check-docs.sh` check 1 (README test count) currently warns and skips when the line is missing — and even when present, it's only checked on demand. Same root cause: nothing auto-runs the linter. The Path B GitHub Action solves both at once.

## What about the README test-count line specifically

The README test-count line ("~1850 tests pass on `main`") is hand-edited per release; check-docs.sh verifies it but doesn't update it. Three options:

1. **Auto-update** in the same workflow — a separate render pass that rewrites the line from `pytest --collect-only` output. More moving parts.
2. **Just enforce** — keep the line hand-edited, but have CI fail on PRs where it's stale (current check-docs.sh behavior, just with the auto-trigger from Path B).
3. **Switch to a soft signal** — replace the precise count with "~1900" and only re-check on order-of-magnitude moves.

Default: **option 2** (just enforce, no auto-update). The line is rarely wrong by more than ±20; precision is editorial. Re-evaluate if it becomes a recurring drift source.

## Related

- [`scripts/check-docs.sh`](https://github.com/jphein/mempalace/blob/main/scripts/check-docs.sh)
- [`scripts/render-docs.py`](https://github.com/jphein/mempalace/blob/main/scripts/render-docs.py)
- [`docs/fork-changes.yaml`](https://github.com/jphein/mempalace/blob/main/docs/fork-changes.yaml)
- `CLAUDE.md` § "Documentation maintenance"
- Companion: [D1] marker-based render insertion for README

","labels":[{"id":"LA_kwDOR-Npq88AAAACetPgMg","name":"documentation","description":"Improvements or additions to documentation","color":"0075ca"}],"number":65,"title":"Auto-trigger render-docs / check-docs on `docs/fork-changes.yaml` changes"},{"body":"## Why this exists

The fork's documentation-maintenance workflow ([CLAUDE.md "Documentation maintenance"](https://github.com/jphein/mempalace/blob/main/CLAUDE.md#documentation-maintenance)) lists four render targets for the canonical `docs/fork-changes.yaml`:

| Target | Status |
|--------|--------|
| `FORK_CHANGELOG.md` | rendered from YAML (today) |
| README fork-change-queue table | hand-maintained for now |
| `scratch/promises.md` (in-repo) | hand-maintained, kept short |
| jphein/mempalace issues | hand-filed as work surfaces |

Only `FORK_CHANGELOG.md` is rendered today. README and the (now-retired) CLAUDE.md row inventory drifted multiple times before the row inventory was retired in [PR #53](https://github.com/jphein/mempalace/pull/53) on 2026-05-12.

The original plan from the canonical-YAML commit (`5a01aec`, 2026-04-26) called out:

> the README fork-change-queue table, this file's row inventory (rows 1–28), and `scratch/promises.md` are still hand-maintained but **planned for marker-based render insertion in a follow-on commit**.

This is that follow-on.

## What to do

Add marker-based render insertion to `scripts/render-docs.py` so the README's fork-change-queue table is regenerated from `docs/fork-changes.yaml` between sentinel markers:

```markdown
<!-- BEGIN: rendered from docs/fork-changes.yaml — do not edit by hand -->
<!-- END: rendered from docs/fork-changes.yaml -->
```

Same pattern for `scratch/promises.md` (if it grows back). CLAUDE.md row inventory is retired — no marker needed there.

`scripts/render-docs.py --target readme` regenerates the table. `--target all` does everything. `--check` mode (already wired for FORK_CHANGELOG.md) extends to the README markers.

## Sequencing

Lower priority than the per-adapter work (Categories A and B above), but worth doing before the next major fork-ahead pile-up. Drift between the YAML and the README table is the next-most-likely place for the documentation surface to lie.

## Out of scope (per current CLAUDE.md note)

- Re-introducing the CLAUDE.md row inventory was **explicitly retired** in PR #53. Don't add it back as a render target.
- The hand-maintained "Fork change queue" in the README's `## Status` section (different from the inline table) is editorial, not data — leave hand-maintained.

## Related

- [`scripts/check-docs.sh`](https://github.com/jphein/mempalace/blob/main/scripts/check-docs.sh) — render-parity check (today: FORK_CHANGELOG.md only)
- [`docs/fork-changes.yaml`](https://github.com/jphein/mempalace/blob/main/docs/fork-changes.yaml) — canonical source
- `CLAUDE.md` § "Documentation maintenance"

","labels":[{"id":"LA_kwDOR-Npq88AAAACetPgMg","name":"documentation","description":"Improvements or additions to documentation","color":"0075ca"}],"number":64,"title":"render-docs.py: marker-based render insertion into README (and `scratch/promises.md`)"},{"body":"## Goal

Track the **RFC 002 §9 cleanup PR** — the prerequisite refactor that moves `miner.py` and `convo_miner.py` onto `BaseSourceAdapter`, routes CLI and MCP through the sources registry, and makes RFC 002 enforceable end-to-end.

This is **upstream work**, not fork work, but we have a direct stake: every other adapter issue we're tracking ([Cursor], [Aider], [Codex/Gemini refactor], [Warp], plus [`#1484`](https://github.com/MemPalace/mempalace/pull/1484)'s CLI wiring) is gated on §9 landing.

## What §9 actually is

From [`docs/rfcs/002-source-adapter-plugin-spec.md`](https://github.com/jphein/mempalace/blob/main/docs/rfcs/002-source-adapter-plugin-spec.md) §9 ("Cleanup prerequisite"):

> The existing in-tree ingesters are not adapter-shaped. Before RFC 002 can be enforced, the following refactor lands in a separate PR:
>
> - Introduce `mempalace/sources/base.py` defining `BaseSourceAdapter`, the typed records, and the registry. ✅ (done in [`#1014`](https://github.com/MemPalace/mempalace/pull/1014))
> - `mempalace/miner.py` → `mempalace/sources/filesystem.py` implementing `BaseSourceAdapter`. **NOT DONE.**
> - `mempalace/convo_miner.py` → `mempalace/sources/conversations.py`. **NOT DONE.**

From RFC 002 §12 (rollout):

> 1. Land the cleanup PR (§9): introduce `mempalace/sources/`, refactor `miner.py` → filesystem adapter, `convo_miner.py` → conversations adapter, route CLI and MCP through the sources registry. **Behavior preserved end-to-end.** Closets get built for conversation drawers as a side effect.

## Current state

- ✅ §9 scaffolding (`base.py`, registry, `PalaceContext`, transforms) landed via [`#1014`](https://github.com/MemPalace/mempalace/pull/1014).
- ✅ First third-party-shaped adapter on the new contract landed via [`#1484`](https://github.com/MemPalace/mempalace/pull/1484) (this fork's OpenCode adapter) — proves the scaffolding works for non-trivial real adapters.
- ❌ **`miner.py` and `convo_miner.py` are still not adapter-shaped.** The §9 cleanup PR has not been filed by anyone.

## Why this fork cares

Without §9 landing:

- `mempalace mine --source <name>` has no CLI route — every new adapter needs its own ad-hoc wiring (see [A2-cli-wiring-opencode]).
- The `if source_type` chain in `normalize.py` keeps growing every time a new format adapter lands (Codex, Gemini are already on it; Aider's #172 will add a sixth branch).
- Closet routing on conversation drawers stays missing — RFC 002 §9 closes it as a side effect.

## What to do

This is a **tracking issue**, not an implementation issue (for now). Watch for upstream signal — likely from @igorls (who shipped #1014 scaffolding) or @bensig (RFC author).

If upstream stalls past ~v3.4: the fork has the conformance suite (`tests/test_sources_opencode.py` is a reasonable template) and could prototype §9 as a fork-side PR for upstream review. Coordination cost is significant — the refactor touches CLI, MCP, miner config, sweeper, hooks. Don't unilaterally start without upstream signal.

## Related

- RFC: [`docs/rfcs/002-source-adapter-plugin-spec.md`](https://github.com/jphein/mempalace/blob/main/docs/rfcs/002-source-adapter-plugin-spec.md) §9 and §12

- [`MemPalace/mempalace#1484`](https://github.com/MemPalace/mempalace/pull/1484) — OpenCode adapter (open) — first non-trivial test of the scaffolding
- [`MemPalace/mempalace#989`](https://github.com/MemPalace/mempalace/issues/989) — RFC 002 tracking issue

RFC 002 source adapter for **Warp** (the terminal), modeled on the OpenCode adapter pattern landing in [`MemPalace/mempalace#1484`](https://github.com/MemPalace/mempalace/pull/1484).

Warp is the last of the seven harnesses called out in the fork README's "What's next" section. Lowest priority — Warp is a terminal with an AI assistant rather than a primary AI coding agent, and its session export format is the least documented of the seven.

## Warp session shape

**Format research is the first task.** Warp sessions ("Workflows" and "AI Command Search" history) are stored locally; the precise on-disk shape is not publicly documented as of 2026-05-12. Candidate locations to investigate:

```
~/.warp/                        # macOS/Linux
~/Library/Application Support/dev.warp.Warp-Stable/  # macOS
```

Likely SQLite based on Warp's electron-ish architecture, but unverified.

Upstream context:

- [`MemPalace/mempalace#59`](https://github.com/MemPalace/mempalace/issues/59) — umbrella ecosystem-import issue **does not list Warp explicitly**; it's in the fork README's "What's next" list but not yet in upstream's table of candidate formats.

No upstream issue or PR exists for Warp. This is greenfield.

## What to do

1. **Format research.** Locate the on-disk session store. Document the schema. Verify it contains the conversational AI history (not just plain terminal output — those are different concerns).
2. **Scope decision.** If Warp's AI history is too thin (e.g., just command suggestions, not multi-turn dialogue), the verbatim-archive value is low and this adapter is deprioritized below Cursor/Aider.
3. **If scope holds**, file an upstream issue mirroring #274 (Cursor's scoping issue) — gauge interest, signal capacity to write the adapter on the #1484 pattern.
4. **Implement** as `mempalace/sources/warp.py` only if upstream signals interest and the format research validates it.

## Sequencing

Lowest priority of the seven. File this so it doesn't drop out of the roadmap, but don't pull it forward of Cursor or Aider unless format research surfaces something unexpectedly compelling.

Track refactoring the **Codex CLI** and **Gemini CLI** session formats — currently shipped as branches inside `mempalace/normalize.py` — onto RFC 002 first-party source adapters, modeled on the OpenCode adapter pattern in [`MemPalace/mempalace#1484`](https://github.com/MemPalace/mempalace/pull/1484).

## Current state

Both are merged into `mempalace/normalize.py` as format-detection branches inside `_try_normalize_json` / `_try_claude_code_jsonl`:

- [`MemPalace/mempalace#61`](https://github.com/MemPalace/mempalace/pull/61) — "feat: add OpenAI Codex CLI JSONL normalizer" (merged 2026-04-07)
- [`MemPalace/mempalace#1234`](https://github.com/MemPalace/mempalace/pull/1234) — "feat(normalize): Gemini CLI session JSONL adapter" (merged 2026-04-27)

Both predate or run in parallel with RFC 002 §9's prescription:

> The format-detection logic in `normalize.py` becomes per-format plugins the conversations adapter composes (one for Claude Code JSONL, one for Codex JSONL, one for ChatGPT mapping trees, one for Claude.ai JSON, one for Slack JSON) — each small and independently testable, eliminating the `if source_type` chain.

Today the `if source_type` chain is the single point of contact between five (now six, with Gemini) hardcoded format detectors. Splitting them is RFC 002 §9's job.

## What to do

This is a **tracking issue**, not an implementation issue — the actual refactor lands as part of §9 cleanup (see separate §9 tracker), not as a separate piece of work. File this so the codex+gemini-specific concern doesn't get forgotten when §9 lands:

- Verify the §9 cleanup PR's per-format plugin list includes **codex** and **gemini-cli** (not just claude-code, chatgpt, claude.ai, slack — RFC 002 §9 as written predates the gemini merge).
- Verify byte-equivalent output for both Codex JSONL and Gemini CLI JSONL before/after the refactor (regression check against the existing test suites for both).
- Verify declared-transformations under §7.3 enumerate everything `normalize.py` does today for each format (especially Codex's tool-call extraction shape and Gemini's `<<<<<<<<< model >>>>>>>>>` delimiter handling).

## Why one issue covers both

Codex and Gemini are the only two normalize.py paths that landed **after** RFC 002 was specced. The other four (Claude Code, ChatGPT, Claude.ai, Slack) predate the RFC; their refactor is squarely §9 cleanup work. Codex and Gemini are the "almost-adapters" — they each could have been adapters but landed as normalize.py branches because §9 wasn't ready. Tracking them together avoids fragmenting the audit.

","labels":[{"id":"LA_kwDOR-Npq88AAAACetPgRw","name":"enhancement","description":"New feature or request","color":"a2eeef"}],"number":61,"title":"Refactor Codex CLI + Gemini CLI normalize.py paths onto RFC 002 adapters"},{"body":"## Goal

RFC 002 source adapter for **Aider**, modeled on the OpenCode adapter pattern landing in [`MemPalace/mempalace#1484`](https://github.com/MemPalace/mempalace/pull/1484).

Aider is one of the seven harnesses called out in the fork README's "What's next" section.

## Aider session shape

Aider stores chat history as a markdown file in the project repo (or a configurable path):

```
<project>/.aider.chat.history.md
```

The format is markdown with conventional `#### user` / `#### assistant` headers and embedded fenced-code-block diffs for edits — not JSON or SQLite. The whole file is one rolling transcript per repo.

Upstream context:

- [`MemPalace/mempalace#172`](https://github.com/MemPalace/mempalace/pull/172) — "feat: add Aider chat history markdown normalizer" by @mvanhorn (OPEN as of 2026-05-12). This is a `normalize.py` patch, not an RFC 002 adapter — predates the spec.
- [`MemPalace/mempalace#59`](https://github.com/MemPalace/mempalace/issues/59) — umbrella ecosystem-import issue lists Aider as in-PR

## What to do

Two paths:

**Path A — help land @mvanhorn's #172 as-is** (`normalize.py` path). This is the smaller change; Aider gets ingested via `mempalace mine --mode convos` like the other normalize-flavored formats. After §9 cleanup lands, the conversations adapter absorbs the normalize.py format-plugin chain and Aider comes along automatically.

**Path B — file a follow-on PR re-shaping Aider as a first-party RFC 002 adapter** (`mempalace/sources/aider.py`). This matches the #1484 OpenCode pattern. Higher coordination cost; only worth it if Path A stalls or upstream signals preference for adapter-first.

Default: **Path A**. Comment on #172 offering review / smoke-test help if it stalls.

## Adapter shape (if Path B)

Mirror [`#1484`](https://github.com/MemPalace/mempalace/pull/1484):
- `source_file`: `aider://<absolute-md-path>#session=<chunk-idx>` (Aider doesn't have stable session IDs; chunk by `#### user` boundary)
- Chunking: exchange-pair (`convo_miner.chunk_exchanges` likely works directly)
- Declared transformations: `utf8_replace_invalid`, `newline_normalize`, `strip_markdown_fence_chrome`, `speaker_role_assignment`
- Default privacy class: `pii_potential`

## Related

- [`MemPalace/mempalace#172`](https://github.com/MemPalace/mempalace/pull/172) — @mvanhorn's normalize.py path (open)
- [`MemPalace/mempalace#1484`](https://github.com/MemPalace/mempalace/pull/1484) — OpenCode adapter pattern (reference)

","labels":[{"id":"LA_kwDOR-Npq88AAAACetPgRw","name":"enhancement","description":"New feature or request","color":"a2eeef"}],"number":59,"title":"RFC 002 source adapter: Aider (markdown chat history)"},{"body":"## Goal

RFC 002 source adapter for **Cursor**, modeled on the OpenCode adapter pattern landing in [`MemPalace/mempalace#1484`](https://github.com/MemPalace/mempalace/pull/1484).

Cursor is one of the seven harnesses called out in the fork README's "What's next" section as targets for first-class adapter support:

> Today's integration is Claude Code-specific … Target the broader set: Claude Code, OpenCode, Cursor, Aider, Gemini CLI, Codex CLI, Warp, and adjacent.

## Cursor session shape

Cursor stores session/workspace state in a per-workspace SQLite database, typically at:

```
~/.config/Cursor/User/workspaceStorage/<workspace-hash>/state.vscdb
~/.cursor/  # legacy / per-user state
```

The `.vscdb` files are SQLite databases (despite the extension) that hold chat history, file edits, and workspace context as VS Code extension state — Cursor inherits VS Code's storage shape and adds its own keys on top.

Upstream context:
- [`MemPalace/mempalace#274`](https://github.com/MemPalace/mempalace/issues/274) — "feat: Native Cursor SQLite Ingestion Support" (open, scoping issue by @Perseusxrltd)
- [`MemPalace/mempalace#245`](https://github.com/MemPalace/mempalace/issues/245) — "Feature: opt-in Cursor source filtering for mempalace_search" (open, by @marerem)
- [`MemPalace/mempalace#59`](https://github.com/MemPalace/mempalace/issues/59) — umbrella ecosystem-import issue lists Cursor as `—` (no PR yet)

No upstream PR exists today — different from Aider (#172), Pi (#169), OpenCode (#23/#1484) which all have working PRs.

## What an adapter needs

Mirror the [`#1484`](https://github.com/MemPalace/mempalace/pull/1484) shape:

- `mempalace/sources/cursor.py` implementing `BaseSourceAdapter`
- `source_file` shape: `cursor://<absolute-vscdb-path>#workspace=<hash>&session=<sid>`
- Chunking: per-message or exchange-pair (likely follow `convo_miner.chunk_exchanges`)
- Declared transformations enumerated under RFC 002 §7.3, every name resolving to a reference impl in `mempalace.sources.transforms`
- Entry-point registration: `cursor = "mempalace.sources.cursor:CursorSourceAdapter"`
- `supports_incremental` + `adapter_owns_routing`
- Default privacy class likely `pii_potential` (chat content includes real code paths, secrets)
- Conformance test suite mirroring `tests/test_sources_opencode.py` (~28 tests)
- SQLite-schema-verbatim fixture in `tests/fixtures/cursor/` (no recorded content)

## Sequencing

Should follow the **RFC 002 §9 cleanup PR** landing first (see separate fork-side tracker). With §9 in place, `mempalace mine --source cursor` works through the same CLI/MCP routing path as opencode and all future adapters.

Whether this is fork-side or upstream is a routing question. Coordinate with @Perseusxrltd on #274 first — if they have an in-flight PR, this becomes a "help land theirs" issue, not a new implementation. If not, this fork is well-positioned to write the adapter on the #1484 pattern (we already have the conformance suite plumbing).

## Related

- [`MemPalace/mempalace#1484`](https://github.com/MemPalace/mempalace/pull/1484) — OpenCode adapter pattern (reference)
- [`MemPalace/mempalace#274`](https://github.com/MemPalace/mempalace/issues/274) — Cursor scoping issue
- [`MemPalace/mempalace#245`](https://github.com/MemPalace/mempalace/issues/245) — Cursor source-filter feature

","labels":[{"id":"LA_kwDOR-Npq88AAAACetPgRw","name":"enhancement","description":"New feature or request","color":"a2eeef"}],"number":58,"title":"RFC 002 source adapter: Cursor (SQLite workspace state)"},{"body":"## Why this exists

[`MemPalace/mempalace#1484`](https://github.com/MemPalace/mempalace/pull/1484) ships `OpenCodeSourceAdapter` registered under `[project.entry-points."mempalace.sources"]` as `opencode`. The adapter is **programmatically reachable** today:

```python
from mempalace.sources.registry import get_adapter
adapter = get_adapter("opencode")
```

…but there is no CLI route. `mempalace mine --source opencode <path>` is not wired through to the registry — it still goes through `convo_miner.py`'s hardcoded format-detection branch. The OpenCode SQLite store is invisible to that branch by design (different file shape, not a transcript).

## What to do

Add CLI wiring so:

```bash
mempalace mine --source opencode ~/.local/share/opencode/opencode.db
mempalace mine --source opencode  # auto-detect default DB path
```

…route to `get_adapter("opencode").ingest(...)` instead of `convo_miner`. The same `--source <name>` flag should work for any registered adapter (`filesystem`, `conversations`, `opencode`, plus whatever future adapters register).

## Sequencing

This is **gated on** the RFC 002 §9 cleanup PR landing first (see [#TBD-§9-tracker], file alongside this one). Per RFC 002 §12 rollout order:

> 1. Land the cleanup PR (§9): introduce `mempalace/sources/`, refactor `miner.py` → filesystem adapter, `convo_miner.py` → conversations adapter, route CLI and MCP through the sources registry.

Once `mempalace mine` is routed through the sources registry, the `--source opencode` path is "free" — no new CLI plumbing per adapter, just registry lookup. Wiring before §9 lands would duplicate work and create a second routing path that §9 then has to unwind.

## Until §9 lands

Programmatic invocation is the workaround. Document the snippet above in adapter-authoring docs (or the per-adapter README on the upstream third-party-package) so early adopters can ingest before CLI wiring exists.

## Related

- Upstream PR: [`MemPalace/mempalace#1484`](https://github.com/MemPalace/mempalace/pull/1484) (OpenCode adapter)
- RFC: [`docs/rfcs/002-source-adapter-plugin-spec.md`](https://github.com/jphein/mempalace/blob/main/docs/rfcs/002-source-adapter-plugin-spec.md) §9 and §12
- Adapter registry: `mempalace/sources/registry.py`

","labels":[{"id":"LA_kwDOR-Npq88AAAACetPgRw","name":"enhancement","description":"New feature or request","color":"a2eeef"}],"number":57,"title":"CLI wiring: `mempalace mine --source opencode` (post-§9 cleanup)"},{"body":"## Why this exists

[`MemPalace/mempalace#1484`](https://github.com/MemPalace/mempalace/pull/1484) (`feat(sources): OpenCode adapter on RFC 002 contract`) ships the first third-party-shaped source adapter on RFC 002. Adapter logic, 28 unit tests, and an RFC 002 §7.3 declared-transformation round-trip all pass against a **SQLite-schema-verbatim synthetic fixture** built by `tests/fixtures/opencode/sample_session_2026_05_12/build_fixture.py`. The fixture mirrors `opencode-ai 1.14.39`'s on-disk schema as captured live from `~/.local/share/opencode/opencode.db` on this host, but **does not contain a real recorded session** — when the fixture was built, opencode was installed with no provider configured, so no real session content was available to record (and real-session content is not sanitizable for upstream redistribution anyway).

The PR's test plan calls this out explicitly:

> - [ ] Real-OpenCode-session smoke (deferred — fixture is schema-verbatim from a real install but doesn't ship recorded content; configuring an OpenCode provider for a billable real-session capture is non-blocking)

## What to do

1. Configure an OpenCode provider on this host (Anthropic API key or local Ollama).
2. Run a real session through opencode that exercises representative tool calls (read, edit, bash) and a few back-and-forth turns.
3. Re-point one of the existing `tests/test_sources_opencode.py` tests at the real `.db` (gated behind an `OPENCODE_REAL_DB` env var, default-skip so CI stays hermetic).
4. Verify the adapter ingests cleanly, drawer shape matches expectations, and transformations round-trip on real content (not just the fixture).
5. Either report a clean smoke in [`#1484`](https://github.com/MemPalace/mempalace/pull/1484) (before merge) or as a follow-up PR if #1484 has already merged.

## Why it's deferred

Configuring an OpenCode provider is billable and host-specific; the synthetic fixture's schema-verbatim guarantee is strong enough to land #1484 on. This is a final-smoke step, not a blocker.

## Related

- Upstream PR: [`MemPalace/mempalace#1484`](https://github.com/MemPalace/mempalace/pull/1484)
- RFC: [`docs/rfcs/002-source-adapter-plugin-spec.md`](https://github.com/jphein/mempalace/blob/main/docs/rfcs/002-source-adapter-plugin-spec.md)
