","labels":[{"id":"LA_kwDOR-Npq88AAAACetPgRw","name":"enhancement","description":"New feature or request","color":"a2eeef"}],"number":56,"title":"Real-OpenCode-session validation smoke for #1484 adapter"},{"body":"## Context

Surfaced 2026-05-11 via P.S. on an external communication ("Would be amped if you can join in") — three issues across two adjacent projects where mempalace's positions are directly relevant. Pattern of recruitment looks like the same channel that brought us [#38](https://github.com/jphein/mempalace/issues/38) (TomLucidor's opencode + oh-my-openagent integration scope from MemPalace/mempalace#1277).

## The three threads

### 1. [anomalyco/opencode#8554](https://github.com/anomalyco/opencode/issues/8554) — "Enable programmatic sub-LLM calls for RLM (Recursive Language Model) pattern"
- Filed 2026-01-14 by BowTiedSwan; 16 comments / 8 unique authors; TomLucidor is in the thread
- Proposes RLM (arxiv 2512.24601) as a built-in tool
- **Our position**: empirical counter-evidence already published. SME jp-realm-v0.1 finding (per README): RLM-Qwen-7B and RLM-Llama-70B both ceiling at 46.67% recall while Familiar's deterministic pipeline hits 78.33%. **RLM doesn't fix retrieval, it just adds a way to recurse.** Engagement could be a short 100-word data-not-opinion comment linking to our methodology disclosure.

### 2. [anomalyco/opencode#11829](https://github.com/anomalyco/opencode/issues/11829) — "RLM Context Management - Context as External Environment"
- Filed 2026-02-02 by chindris-mihai-alexandru; 1 comment (CI only); builds on opencode#4659
- Frames RLM as "context as external environment the model queries programmatically" — **conceptually equivalent to what MCP + mempalace_search already deliver**
- Lower priority (dead-ish thread), but a single comment pointing at our shipping implementation would land.

### 3. [code-yeongyu/oh-my-openagent#1397](https://github.com/code-yeongyu/oh-my-openagent/issues/1397) — "[RFE]: Automated Learning Capture System"
- Filed 2026-02-02 by agno01; **59 comments, 16 unique authors** — most active of the three; TomLucidor is here too
- Proposes auto-detecting, classifying, validating, and persisting session learnings into AGENTS.md / CLAUDE.md / skills via GitOps
- **Direct conflict with our pivot**: PR [#673](https://github.com/MemPalace/mempalace/pull/673) (silent deterministic saves, no LLM in the write path), our `feedback_promises_tracker.md` rewrite this session, the github-issues-for-durable-work shift — all argue the opposite: AI-summarized learning is non-deterministic and lossy; verbatim is the design test.
- Engagement needs a real position post, not a drive-by. Worth a separate scoping issue if we decide to engage seriously.

## Decision points (don't decide now)

- Engage 8554 with the RLM empirical counter? ~100 words; cost low; could land same day.
- Comment on 11829 pointing at our MCP-as-external-environment shipping implementation? ~50 words; cost trivial.
- Position post on 1397? Substantial; would need to coordinate with our existing public stance (README four-layer framing, the Auto Dream vindication framing).
- All of the above tie to [#47](https://github.com/jphein/mempalace/issues/47) "Publish standalone essay on the verbatim-vs-derivative axis" — if that essay lands first, every comment on these three threads becomes "see [link]."

## Status

Open. No immediate action — JP's mempalace install fix takes priority. Revisit after install settles, or after #47 (verbatim-vs-derivative essay) is drafted.

Source: 2026-05-11 session, post-cleanup, P.S. on external message.","labels":[],"number":54,"title":"Engage in opencode RLM + oh-my-openagent learning-capture threads"},{"body":"## Context

The fork hit a real production failure on 2026-05-10 (documented in [familiar.realm.watch's CHANGELOG](https://github.com/jphein/familiar.realm.watch/blob/main/CHANGELOG.md) under "foundation rework — kill the split-brain"): a daemon-deployed setup silently routed Stop-hook writes to a local palace instead of the daemon's. The smoking gun was that `mempalace`'s `_daemon_strict()` callsites check **only** the `PALACE_DAEMON_URL` env var — if it doesn't propagate to a hook/MCP subprocess, writes silently land locally.

## What's not a bug

The current design IS intentional opt-in:

- No `PALACE_DAEMON_URL` → run locally → correct default for upstream's expected single-host install
- `PALACE_DAEMON_URL` set → route through daemon

That's the right shape for upstream's audience. It's NOT a code bug.

## What is a usability gap (specific to our deployment shape)

In a daemon-deployed setup (mempalace on katana, palace-daemon on disks), the env var must propagate to every spawned subprocess that calls into mempalace:

- The `mempalace-mcp` process Claude Code spawns
- Direct CLI invocations of `mempalace search / status / mine`
- (Previously) `mempal-stop-hook.sh` — no longer relevant post-PR-#27 since hook routing now goes through palace-daemon's `hook.py` client (which reads `hook_settings.json` correctly)

When the env var doesn't propagate (Claude Code spawn context, scoped settings, plugin layer behavior, manual `env -i` invocation), the user sees green status, writes succeed locally, and the daemon view shows missing recent content. Diagnosing takes hours.

## Why this is fork-internal first (not upstream-first)

Most upstream users run single-host: the env-var-only signal is fine. The fork hits this because we deliberately split palace-daemon onto disks. The right place to develop the fix is the fork. If it pans out, the eventual upstream contribution has real production credibility ("ran on our fork for N months across these deployment shapes; recommend adopting").

## Proposed implementation

Three small changes, additive, default-preserving:

### 1. `MempalaceConfig.daemon_url` property (new)

Mirror the env > config-file > default resolution shape `palace_path` already uses:

```python
@property
def daemon_url(self):
    """Optional daemon URL for routing. Env var wins; config.json key
    'daemon_url' as fallback; None means run locally (current default)."""
    env_val = os.environ.get("PALACE_DAEMON_URL", "").strip()
    if env_val:
        return env_val
    return self._file_config.get("daemon_url") or None
```

Optional companion properties: `daemon_strict` (default True when daemon_url is set), `daemon_api_key` (optional auth).

### 2. Update `_daemon_strict()` callsites to consult config-file fallback

- `mempalace/mcp_server.py:279`
- `mempalace/cli.py:63`

Both should resolve daemon_url via `MempalaceConfig().daemon_url` instead of `os.environ.get("PALACE_DAEMON_URL")` directly. Env var still wins; config-file fallback fills the gap.

The hooks_cli.py callsite stays env-only (hooks now route through palace-daemon's hook.py which has its own `hook_settings.json` lookup; the mempalace-side hooks_cli stays as the fallback for the rare bash-script direct invocation).

### 3. Emit one log line at MCP server + CLI startup

```
mempalace-mcp: routing → daemon @ http://disks.jphe.in:8085
```
or
```
mempalace-mcp: routing → local palace @ /home/jp/.mempalace/palace
```

Makes the routing decision audible the first time it happens. The user notices the split-brain immediately instead of after a month of missing writes.

## Precedent

palace-daemon's `clients/hook.py` (line 41+) already implements this pattern: reads `~/.mempalace/hook_settings.json` for `daemon_url` with `http://localhost:8085` as default. That file lives under mempalace's namespace but is consumed by palace-daemon — which is itself a coupling smell. Mempalace's CLI + MCP server adopting the same config-file lookup would close the loop cleanly.

## Open question

Where does the config-file value live? Two paths:

- (a) `~/.mempalace/config.json` with a new `daemon_url` key — keeps mempalace's config in one place
- (b) `~/.mempalace/hook_settings.json` — shared with palace-daemon's hook.py (already exists)

(a) is cleaner separation; (b) avoids fragmentation. Lean (a) since palace-daemon's hook_settings.json is an implementation detail of its hook client.

## Why a feature, not a bug

The current code is correct as-designed. Adding the fallback is a UX/ergonomics enhancement specific to multi-host deployments. The bug-shaped framing was wrong on the original scratch draft (`scratch/upstream-issue-draft.md`, written 2026-05-10 pre-PR-#27).

## Related

- Familiar's CHANGELOG "foundation rework" section
- Fork commits: `41359ba` (mcp_server daemon-strict), `22ef562` (cli daemon-strict), `42ded2e` (hooks promote to palace-daemon hook.py via PR #27)
- palace-daemon's `clients/hook.py:41` (HOOK_SETTINGS_PATH constant)
- `scratch/upstream-issue-draft.md` (now redundant — content captured here)","labels":[{"id":"LA_kwDOR-Npq88AAAACetPgRw","name":"enhancement","description":"New feature or request","color":"a2eeef"}],"number":49,"title":"Add MempalaceConfig.daemon_url config-file fallback + log routing decision at CLI/MCP startup"},{"body":"## Context

The fork's README leads with the four-layer model (storage / encoder / retrieval / consumption). One foundational claim threads through it: **the unit of memory in MemPalace is the verbatim utterance. Anything else — summaries, KG triples, agent journals, AAAK-encoded reflections, Auto-Dream consolidated indices — is *derivative* of that verbatim record.**

That axis got the strongest external validation today when Anthropic's Dreams API design (input read-only, output a separate store you review/attach/discard) landed on the same architectural call. Worth its own dedicated piece, separate from the README's tactical thesis section.

## What to write

Standalone essay covering:

1. **The architectural choice**: store verbatim, derive lazily, derivative-replaceable
2. **Why the alternative (derive-on-write) accumulates failure modes**: lost nuance, classifier ceiling, irreversibility, locked-in to the assumption your derivation algorithm is right
3. **Empirical signal**: the recovery-collection split (Apr 25 → May 5, 2026) — closed a 210× token gap when corpus shape was fixed at write time
4. **Vendor-API validation**: Anthropic's Dreams API design as ratification
5. **Operational implications**: backup, audit, re-derivation, model-upgrade resilience
6. **Limits**: when verbatim DOES need explicit shelving (`forget this` action — separate from decay), per-domain considerations

## Where to publish

- `docs/research/verbatim-vs-derivative-axis.md` (fork-side)
- Cross-post or summarize on JP's blog/website (jphein.com or similar)
- Cite from README's thesis section as the deeper read

## Why this matters

README compresses the argument into 3 principles + a four-layer model. The essay can develop the verbatim-vs-derivative axis specifically — explain WHY the choice matters, what the alternatives are, and what evidence supports the call. Different surface than the README; longer-form analytic.

## Related
- README "What's next" → verbatim-vs-derivative essay
- Anthropic's [Dreams API docs](https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/managed-agents/dreams)","labels":[{"id":"LA_kwDOR-Npq88AAAACetPgMg","name":"documentation","description":"Improvements or additions to documentation","color":"0075ca"}],"number":47,"title":"Publish standalone essay on the verbatim-vs-derivative axis"},{"body":"## Context

[RFC 001 (PR #743)](https://github.com/MemPalace/mempalace/pull/743) defines the backend abstraction layer mempalace now ships on top of. The implementation supports multi-collection-per-palace today (`get_collection(palace, collection_name=...)` takes a name), but the spec doesn't explicitly name this pattern as an architectural commitment.

Implicit support → explicit pattern is worth a small spec amendment because:

1. New backends (pgvector + AGE in flight; sqlite_vec from @MohamedAbdallah-14's #1386) need to know the contract: every collection must be independently get-able, deletable, queryable
2. Multi-collection-by-purpose is the canonical answer to several open design questions (kostadis's #1018, P8 corpus partitioning, KG store separation)
3. Read-surface parity is a precondition the fork learned the hard way (recovery-collection split retired May 5, 2026 because the recovery side never got a semantic-search MCP read tool)

## What to propose

A small follow-up note to RFC 001 (or a follow-up RFC) capturing:

- **Multi-collection-per-palace is a first-class pattern**, not just an implementation detail
- **Read-surface parity is a precondition**: a new sibling collection must have an MCP read tool before write paths populate it
- **Backend contract**: every collection must be independently get/delete/query-able through the BaseBackend interface; backends MAY enforce this with separate physical storage (Postgres schemas, separate Chroma collections) or virtual partitioning (metadata key tag)
- **Backend capability declaration**: collections `is_searchable` flag, etc.

## How to propose

1. Read the merged RFC 001 spec text
2. Draft the follow-up as a Discussion (rather than PR) to surface the question to maintainers
3. Wait for maintainer agreement on shape before filing a spec-amendment PR

## Related
- [RFC 001 PR #743](https://github.com/MemPalace/mempalace/pull/743) (merged)
- README "What's next" → coordinate with upstream on multi-collection-by-purpose pattern
- Adjacent to issue #15 (multi-palace design)","labels":[{"id":"LA_kwDOR-Npq88AAAACetPgRw","name":"enhancement","description":"New feature or request","color":"a2eeef"}],"number":46,"title":"Coordinate with upstream on naming multi-collection-by-purpose pattern (RFC 001 follow-up)"},{"body":"## Context

@kostadis raised in upstream [discussion #1018](https://github.com/MemPalace/mempalace/discussions/1018): a manually curated palace alongside the auto-mined chat palace. The hooks dump everything into one palace today, polluting curated content with session ephemera.

## Design questions

The single-`palace_path` model assumes one palace per process. Multi-palace needs:

1. Does it live as multiple `palace_path` values (config-listed), or one path with named aliases?
2. Per-hook target flag — Stop-hook writes to chat-palace, manual mine writes to authority-palace
3. Search surface: query all palaces? query a named one? interleave results?
4. Daemon routing: one daemon per palace, or one daemon multi-palace?
5. Index: shared embedding model across palaces, but separate HNSW segments
6. CLI:  `mempalace search --palace=authority "query"`

## Alternative path: P8 corpus partitioning within a single palace

The recovery-collection split (Apr 25 → May 5, 2026) was the first attempt at this — sibling collections inside one palace, per-purpose. Retired May 5 because the recovery collection never got a semantic-search MCP surface (the partition was write-side without the read-side parity). README P8 entry says: "each new sibling collection has to earn its own read tool before it gets writes."

Same architectural question, different shape: is multi-palace a packaging convenience over what multi-collection-per-palace would also enable?

## Recommendation

Try collection-partitioning first (P8 path, smaller change), see if it absorbs the authority-vs-mined distinction. If it doesn't — gaps in cross-collection search, scope-management complexity — escalate to multi-palace as a separate config surface.

## Related
- Upstream discussion [#1018](https://github.com/MemPalace/mempalace/discussions/1018)
- README "Active investigations" → "Multi-palace separation"
- README P8 (corpus partitioning by purpose) — on hold pending design clarity","labels":[{"id":"LA_kwDOR-Npq88AAAACetPgkQ","name":"question","description":"Further information is requested","color":"d876e3"}],"number":45,"title":"Design: multi-palace separation — curated 'authority' palace vs auto-mined chat palace"},{"body":"## What

Today's `mempalace` CLI is operator-shaped (status / mine / repair / search) — returns human-readable text. The next surface is **agent-shaped**: structured JSON output, exit codes that mean something, conventions that compose with shell pipelines and slash commands.

Pattern reference: [Grafana's GCX CLI](https://www.infoq.com/news/2026/04/grafana-loki-ai-agents/) — bring the data to where the agent lives, don't force the agent into a separate UI.

## Surface to build

```bash
mempalace search "query" --json | jq '.results[0]'
mempalace search "query" --json --limit 5 --since 30d
mempalace status --json
mempalace mined --json --wing=project_x
mempalace verify --json  # composes with /verify-docs from #13
```

JSON shape mirrors the existing MCP `mempalace_search` response: `results: [{drawer_id, wing, room, content, similarity, ...}]`, plus top-level `warnings`, `available_in_scope`, `query`.

## Why this matters

- MCP brings palace into Claude Code via tool calls; CLI brings palace into shell pipelines, slash commands, cron, ops scripts
- Non-Claude-Code agents (opencode, codex, gemini-cli, aider) can use CLI before MCP source-adapter exists for them
- Hooks can shell out to CLI from any harness that doesn't have native MCP support

## Approach

1. Audit existing CLI commands; identify which already produce structured output internally (status, search, mined)
2. Add `--json` flag that suppresses prose chrome and emits the underlying data structure
3. Exit codes: 0 = success, 1 = no results, 2 = palace unavailable, 64 = bad args
4. Stable JSON schema documented at `docs/reference/cli-json-schema.md`

## Related
- README "What's next" → agent-shaped CLI surface
- Composes with #38 (multi-agent ecosystem integration) — CLI is the universal substrate before per-agent source-adapters exist","labels":[{"id":"LA_kwDOR-Npq88AAAACetPgRw","name":"enhancement","description":"New feature or request","color":"a2eeef"}],"number":44,"title":"Agent-shaped CLI surface — pipe-friendly structured output for non-MCP integration"},{"body":"## Problem

Knowledge lives across 7+ layers in this project: global CLAUDE.md, project CLAUDE.md, auto-memory, docs/, superpowers specs, code comments, MemPalace. The auto-loaded layers go stale and actively mislead. MemPalace is the only layer that *can't* go stale (verbatim + timestamped) but is never auto-loaded.

Stale-doc-induced wrong assumptions are a meaningful failure mode of agent workflows.

## What

Slash command `/verify-docs` that pattern-matches:

- Version strings: `v?\d+\.\d+\.\d+` against `mempalace/version.py` or `pyproject.toml`
- File paths: walk paths in docs and check existence
- PR numbers: `#\d+` against `gh pr view N --repo MemPalace/mempalace --json state`
- Upstream commit hashes: against `git cat-file -e`
- URLs: HEAD-check (with a stoplist for known-flaky external sites)
- Test counts: against `pytest --collect-only` or recent CI
- Drawer counts: against palace-daemon `/stats`

Outputs:
- Pass/fail for each check
- Diff-style suggestions ("v3.3.4 → v3.3.5")
- Optionally: auto-fix obvious ones via PR

## Why this matters

Cleaning stale docs prevents more wrong assumptions than any amount of auto-querying. Anchor: this fork's own README has been wrong about Auto Dream's release status for ~3 weeks before today's fix.

## Approach

1. Walk doc files (CLAUDE.md, README.md, FORK_CHANGELOG.md, docs/**.md)
2. Apply pattern detectors
3. Report findings
4. Optional auto-fix with operator confirmation

## Related
- README "Active investigations" → "Stale auto-loaded docs"","labels":[{"id":"LA_kwDOR-Npq88AAAACetPgRw","name":"enhancement","description":"New feature or request","color":"a2eeef"}],"number":43,"title":"/verify-docs slash command — pattern-match version strings + URLs against current state"},{"body":"## Context

[multipass-structural-memory-eval](https://github.com/M0nkeyFl0wer/multipass-structural-memory-eval) is the Nine-category SME framework. Cat 9 (The Handshake) is the integration gap our work has been operationalizing. Forked at [jphein/multipass-structural-memory-eval](https://github.com/jphein/multipass-structural-memory-eval).

## What to build

Adapters for: [Longhand](https://glama.ai/mcp/servers/Wynelson94/longhand), [Celiums](https://celiums.ai/), [mcp-memory-service](https://github.com/doobidoo/mcp-memory-service). Each:

The structural-shift work from 2026-04-25 → 2026-04-26 (recovery-collection split) closed the checkpoint-domination instance of the Cat 9 integration gap documented by [engram-2](https://github.com/199-biotechnologies/engram-2). - Gated by #31 (rebuild speed) since rebuilds enable iterating on the corpus shape","labels":[{"id":"LA_kwDOR-Npq88AAAACetPgMg","name":"documentation","description":"Improvements or additions to documentation","color":"0075ca"}],"number":41,"title":"Publish Cat 9 / The Handshake end-to-end results from post-migration palace"},{"body":"## What

Strip known injection patterns from drawer content on write. Flag with `sanitized: true` metadata when modified; **don't block** — sanitization is observation-grade, not gate-grade. Apply a 10K char cap on individual chunks.

Per README "Planned work" P6: low priority while local-only.

## Why low priority

The fork operates entirely on-host (Claude Code session JSONL → palace via daemon on JP's homelab LAN). Adversarial content doesn't reach the palace unless JP types it. Sanitization is defensive but not urgent.

## When this changes

If/when the palace surface goes multi-user or accepts content from untrusted sources (e.g., shared team palace, external agent ingest), this becomes mandatory rather than nice-to-have.

## Approach (small)

- One helper `_sanitize_drawer_content` in mining path, applied to chunk text before embedding
- Flag the drawer with `{"sanitized": true}` if patterns matched; don't change the content
- 10K char cap with explicit truncation flag
- Half day, additive

## Related
- README "Planned work" / P6","labels":[{"id":"LA_kwDOR-Npq88AAAACetPgRw","name":"enhancement","description":"New feature or request","color":"a2eeef"}],"number":40,"title":"P6: Input sanitization on writes (low priority, local-only)"},{"body":"## What

Add `tags` metadata (3-8 per drawer) extracted during mining via TF-IDF or longest-non-stopword heuristic. Tags are the cross-cutting-concerns layer that wing/room hierarchy can't represent — a drawer about "pgvector deployment in homelab" lives in one wing/room but is genuinely about both *infrastructure* AND *pgvector* AND *deployment*.

Adjacent: upstream [#1033](https://github.com/MemPalace/mempalace/pull/1033) (@zackchiutw, `<private>` tag filter) is single-purpose. Full multi-label additive on top.

## Sizing

1-2 days, additive (per README "Planned work" P0 entry).

## Approach

1. Extend drawer metadata schema with `tags: list[str]` (3-8 entries, lowercase-normalized).
2. Tag extraction during mining: longest-non-stopword candidates from chunk; TF-IDF prune to top N.
3. Optional opt-in `--enrich` flag for Haiku-extracted topic tags (96.6% R@5 baseline → competitive before rerank — relatively cheap LLM call per file).
4. MCP search surface: extend `mempalace_search` with `tags=[...]` filter (AND-of-OR-of: each tag matches if ANY listed value found).
5. Tests: tagging unit + scope-filter end-to-end + Haiku-enrichment opt-in.

## Why now-ish

- Decay (P2) is tracked upstream — frees fork bandwidth for cross-cutting concerns
- Postgres + pgvector substrate makes JSONB tag queries cheap and fast
- Composes with #1033 if that merges first

## Related
- README "Planned work" / P0
- Upstream #1033","labels":[{"id":"LA_kwDOR-Npq88AAAACetPgRw","name":"enhancement","description":"New feature or request","color":"a2eeef"}],"number":39,"title":"P0: Multi-label tags — additive cross-cutting layer that hierarchy can't provide"},{"body":"## Why this issue exists

In MemPalace/mempalace [discussion #1277](https://github.com/MemPalace/mempalace/discussions/1277), @TomLucidor surfaced three issues asking about MemPalace integration with other agent harnesses:

- [anomalyco/opencode#8554](https://github.com/anomalyco/opencode/issues/8554)
- [anomalyco/opencode#11829](https://github.com/anomalyco/opencode/issues/11829)
- [code-yeongyu/oh-my-openagent#1397](https://github.com/code-yeongyu/oh-my-openagent/issues/1397)

In the [reply](https://github.com/MemPalace/mempalace/discussions/1277#discussioncomment-16884950) I committed to taking a look at these issues. Tracking that promise here so it doesn't slip.

## Roadmap context

The fork's README "What's next" section calls out **first-class support across the AI coding agent ecosystem** — Claude Code, OpenCode, Cursor, Aider, Gemini CLI, Codex CLI, Warp. Integration path goes through upstream's [RFC 002 source-adapter spec](https://github.com/MemPalace/mempalace/pull/990) (tracking [#989](https://github.com/MemPalace/mempalace/issues/989)).

Today's integration is Claude-Code-specific (Stop / PreCompact hooks reading `~/.claude/projects/*.jsonl`, mining via palace-daemon). The model going forward: each new agent ships a `pip install mempalace-source-<agent>` package mapping its session format onto the canonical drawer shape.

## What to do (in priority order)

1. Read the three external issues to understand what integration shape is being asked for
2. Identify whether opencode + oh-my-openagent fit the RFC 002 source-adapter pattern, or need different integration shape
3. If they fit: write a one-pager design note for `mempalace-source-opencode` and one for `-omoa`. Park as planning.
4. If they don't fit cleanly: document the gap and suggest the right pattern to those projects' maintainers

## Promises tracked

- `scratch/promises.md` Active table now has a row for this commitment
- Per `feedback_audit_comments_with_triage_perms` rule 4: needs fulfillment OR edit-the-promise-out of the discussion comment

- Discussion comment that committed to this: https://github.com/MemPalace/mempalace/discussions/1277#discussioncomment-16884950","labels":[{"id":"LA_kwDOR-Npq88AAAACetPgRw","name":"enhancement","description":"New feature or request","color":"a2eeef"}],"number":38,"title":"Track opencode + oh-my-openagent integration evaluation (multi-agent ecosystem roadmap)"},{"body":"## Context

When cherry-picking #665 in PR #21, the backend interface changed from `get_collection(palace_path, collection_name, ...)` to `get_collection(palace=PalaceRef(id=palace_path, local_path=palace_path), collection_name=..., create=..., options=...)`.

The PR #21 cherry-pick handled `palace.get_collection` and `searcher.search_memories` call sites, plus the test fixture. But `mempalace/cli.py` still has these call sites I didn't audit:

- `cli.py:1043`: `backend.get_collection(palace_path, "mempalace_drawers")`
- `cli.py:1133`: `backend.get_collection(palace_path, "mempalace_drawers")`
- `cli.py:1343`: `backend.get_collection(palace_path, collection_name)`
- `cli.py:1490`: `backend.get_collection(palace_path, "mempalace_drawers")`

These use the **old positional** signature. The test suite is green (1854 passed last full run with TEST_POSTGRES_DSN set), so either:

(a) ChromaBackend retained backward-compat for positional args
(b) These code paths aren't exercised by any test
(c) Something else

## What to do

1. Determine which of (a)/(b)/(c) is true — probably read `ChromaBackend.get_collection` and check whether it has positional fallback or kwarg-only
2. If (a): document the backward-compat explicitly; otherwise migrate to the new signature
3. If (b): add test coverage so the migration is verifiable
4. Migrate the 4 sites to `backend.get_collection(palace=PalaceRef(...), collection_name=..., create=...)` for consistency with the rest of the codebase

## Related

- PR [#21](https://github.com/jphein/mempalace/pull/21) cherry-pick
- HANDOFF.md notes this as a follow-up

So our cherry-pick is now functionally redundant with what's on develop. The row's claim is stale.

When cherry-picking upstream #665 in PR [#21](https://github.com/jphein/mempalace/pull/21) (commit `5e90c72`), the upstream PR removed the module-level `_DEFAULT_BACKEND = ChromaBackend()` symbol in `mempalace/palace.py` because `get_collection` now routes through `resolve_backend_for_palace`.

But fork-side callers in `mempalace/mcp_server.py` still treat the default backend as a module attribute:
- Line 204-207: `palace._DEFAULT_BACKEND._clients.pop(...)` / `._freshness.pop(...)` (cache invalidation)
- Line 1703: `palace_module._DEFAULT_BACKEND.close_palace(_config.palace_path)`

And tests:
- `tests/test_backends.py:1699`: `monkeypatch.setattr(palace._DEFAULT_BACKEND, "get_collection", ...)`
- `tests/test_mcp_server.py:1466`: `monkeypatch.setattr(palace._DEFAULT_BACKEND, "close_palace", ...)`

To keep all five sites working, the cherry-pick added a transitional shim: `_DEFAULT_BACKEND = get_backend("chroma")` at module level in `palace.py`. The default-backend singleton routes correctly to chroma, so the existing callers continue to function — but the shim is fork-only and creates technical debt.

## What to do

1. Migrate the 3 production call sites in `mcp_server.py` to use `get_backend("chroma")` directly (or a typed wrapper if the surface is too wide)
2. Update the 2 test monkeypatches to target `get_backend("chroma")` instead of the module attribute
3. Once all 5 callers are migrated: delete the shim from `palace.py` and the explanatory comment block

## Why this matters

- Reduces fork-ahead diff (one less line of fork-only adapter code)
- Removes a transitional comment that future readers have to parse
- Sets cleaner foundation for any new backend (postgres, sqlite_vec) that wants similar cache-invalidation semantics

## Related

- PR [#21](https://github.com/jphein/mempalace/pull/21) — the cherry-pick that introduced the shim
- Commit `5e90c72` body documents the shim
- CLAUDE.md row 21 (pgvector-substrate row) notes 'follow-up commits will migrate callers'","labels":[{"id":"LA_kwDOR-Npq88AAAACetPgRw","name":"enhancement","description":"New feature or request","color":"a2eeef"}],"number":35,"title":"Migrate mcp_server.py + tests off the _DEFAULT_BACKEND compat shim added in PR #21"},{"body":"## What's done

- Task 2.1 (commit `a3ee623`, PR [#25](https://github.com/jphein/mempalace/pull/25)): `KnowledgeGraphAGE` skeleton with `mempalace_kg` graph bootstrap on Apache AGE; 3 skipif-gated tests passing against the live `mempalace-db` container on disks.

## What's remaining

Per `docs/superpowers/plans/2026-05-10-pgvector-age-migration-impl.md`:

- **Task 2.2** — `add_triple()` via Cypher MERGE/CREATE. Substantive: entity vertex labels, predicate edge labels, property semantics, idempotent re-insert. Tests for add + read-back.
- **Task 2.3** — Temporal filtering (`as_of` queries). Builds on 2.2; needs interval columns or Cypher pattern for valid_from / valid_to (AGE doesn't have native interval types — needs design).
- **Task 2.4** — `MempalaceConfig.kg_backend` flag + routing. Small wiring change once 2.2 + 2.3 are stable; lets `MEMPALACE_KG_BACKEND=age` actually select the new backend over the SQLite default.

## CI image gating

When Task 2.2's first `add_triple` test lands, the CI postgres image needs an AGE upgrade. Current image is `pgvector/pgvector:pg16` (no AGE). Options:

1. Push our `mempalace-db:0.1` derived image to `ghcr.io/jphein/mempalace-db` and reference as the service container
2. Build inline in CI (slower, no registry write)
3. Extend the `TEST_POSTGRES_DSN` skipif to also check for AGE presence — keeps tests gated on operator-provided substrate

Recommendation: option 3 until AGE is exercised by multiple tests, then option 1 for stability.","labels":[{"id":"LA_kwDOR-Npq88AAAACetPgRw","name":"enhancement","description":"New feature or request","color":"a2eeef"}],"number":34,"title":"Phase 2 (AGE knowledge-graph layer) — tasks 2.2, 2.3, 2.4 remaining"},{"body":"## Context

@dergachoff's open upstream PR [#1452](https://github.com/MemPalace/mempalace/pull/1452) refines `quarantine_invalid_hnsw_metadata()` so segments with `dimensionality=None` aren't quarantined when the rest of the segment looks otherwise recoverable (sane HNSW payload, matching element counts, bidirectional label maps).

Closes upstream #1451.

## Why relevant to the fork

Today's repair journal on `disks` showed multiple segments with `HNSW mtime gap` warnings that the current daemon code left in place ("Leaving in place"). #1452's refinement would make those decisions more accurate going forward — fewer false positives on quarantine, healthier segment retention.

## What to do

1. Wait for #1452 to settle (gemini bot review only at present; no maintainer engagement yet)
2. If urgent need surfaces during another rebuild, cherry-pick onto fork main as a no-op-on-merge entry
3. Otherwise let it merge upstream and pick up via next develop sync

## Related

- Fatkobra's open [#1461](https://github.com/MemPalace/mempalace/pull/1461) (link_lists.bin integrity gate) — orthogonal: #1461 fixes false-negative quarantine; #1452 fixes false-positive
- Both touch the same quarantine logic but at different gates","labels":[{"id":"LA_kwDOR-Npq88AAAACetPgRw","name":"enhancement","description":"New feature or request","color":"a2eeef"}],"number":33,"title":"Evaluate cherry-pick of upstream #1452 (avoid quarantining recoverable HNSW metadata)"},{"body":"## Symptom

The defense-in-depth sanitizers in:
- `mempalace/repair.py:_extract_drawers` (commit `949cb20`)
- `mempalace/repair.py:_rebuild_one_collection` (same commit)
- `mempalace/backends/chroma.py:add/upsert` (commit `f499814` — the chokepoint catch-all)

…all coerce None and empty-dict entries to `{"_repaired_empty_meta": True}`. Yet the 151K-drawer rebuild was still failing at ~120K with the same `ValueError: Expected metadata to be a non-empty dict, got 0 metadata attributes in add`.

The traceback ran through: `mempalace/backends/chroma.py:add → chromadb Collection.add → validate_insert_record_set → validate_metadatas → validate_metadata`.

## Hypothesis (from the f499814 commit message)

Something between the repair-layer sanitizer and chromadb's actual write call is reshaping the metadatas list. Most plausible candidates:

1. chromadb's `upsert` internally splits into add+update paths and one of those splits drops/replaces metadatas
2. A deeper preprocessing step in chromadb (`validate_insert_record_set` itself? or before?) introduces empty dicts from sparse columns
3. Our own `_sanitize_metadatas_for_chromadb` runs on the original list but chromadb iterates a copy that goes stale

## Why this matters

f499814 is a known-symptom-fix, not a root-cause-fix. The chokepoint sanitizer is operationally fine (one list comprehension per write, negligible cost) but it's catching a bug we don't understand. Root-cause clarity lets us:
- Drop one of the three sanitizer layers (the right one to drop is whichever's actually redundant given the real reshape point)
- Pitch the fix upstream at the correct architectural layer (currently the chokepoint sanitizer is fork-only, defensible but not the cleanest upstream contribution)
- Predict similar failures across other backends (postgres, sqlite_vec, etc.)

## Suggested approach

1. Reproduce on a small palace (~1K drawers) with deliberately corrupted metadata
2. Instrument `ChromaCollection.add` to log `metadatas` state immediately before `self._raw.add(**kwargs)`
3. Set a breakpoint or pdb trace inside chromadb's `validate_metadata` to see what list reaches it
4. Diff the two

## Related

- Defense-in-depth commit: `f499814` (fork-only, direct-to-main)
- Repair sanitizers: `949cb20` (jphein PR #28, upstream PR #1459)
- CLAUDE.md row 39 documents the chokepoint sanitizer rationale","labels":[{"id":"LA_kwDOR-Npq88AAAACetPgJA","name":"bug","description":"Something isn't working","color":"d73a4a"}],"number":32,"title":"Root-cause: what reshapes metadatas between repair-layer sanitizer and chromadb's validate_metadata?"},{"body":"","labels":[],"number":17,"title":"either integrate the knowledge graph or switch to postgres with PG vector and star"},{"body":"","labels":[],"number":16,"title":"rebase PRs"},{"body":"https://thenewstack.io/pinecone-nexus-rag-obsolete/","labels":[],"number":13,"title":"rag and pinecone Nexus"}]

name: palace-room-taxonomy-project-topic-drawer-with-7-canonical-rooms
description: "JP approved a rigid wing/room model for the palace. Wing=project, Room∈{architecture, decisions, problems, planning, sessions, references, discoveries}, Drawer=entry, Closet=auto-built index. Enforced at pgvector migration, not retroactively."

  node_type: memory

> "palace ├─ mempalace (wing) │  ├─ architecture (room) │  ├─ problems (room) │  ├─ sessions (room) │  └─ (closets auto-built)"
The next message would propose a canonical room list (e.g. architecture, problems, sessions, decisions, planning, references) and ask JP to confirm/edit before we make this a design doc. Given the migration choice is "Postgres migration first", the design lives as a written spec until pgvector migration starts — no immediate code changes needed. Suggested specific next step: "Propose a canonical room list with ~6-8 recurring rooms (architecture, problems, sessions, decisions, planning, references, etc.) and write a design doc capturing the Project-Topic-Drawer model for the pgvector migration to embody, without touching code today." If you need specific details fr

**Familiar's modality landscape:** The canonical 7 rooms already serve as a clean modality proxy — references/problems/sessions are verbatim source material (4,303 drawers), architecture/decisions/planning/discoveries are derivative/summary content (173 drawers), diary is compressed. No additional per-drawer metadata needed. The KG modality is effectively nonexistent (50K entities but only 1 triple in AGE).

**The 7 canonical rooms** — closed set, never invent new ones:
1. `architecture` — how things are built (current shape)
2. `decisions` — explicit choices with rationale (atemporal)
3. `problems` — bugs, incidents, debugging trails
4. `planning` — roadmaps, plans, what comes next
5. `sessions` — chronological journal entries
6. `references` — pointers to external systems, runbooks, dashboards
7. `discoveries` — durable lessons not tied to a single incident

---

## How Palace Mode Works (`--mode palace`)

Palace mode is a structural upgrade that uses the full MemPal hall/wing/closet/drawer architecture for retrieval. Instead of searching everything flat, it navigates into the most likely hall first, then falls back to the full haystack with hall-aware scoring.

### The Palace Structure

```
PALACE
  └── HALL (content type: preferences / facts / events / assistant_advice / general)
        └── CLOSET (user turns per session — the primary index)
              └── DRAWER (assistant turns — opened on demand for assistant-reference questions)
  └── PREFERENCE WING (synthetic docs extracted from user expressions — separate from halls)
```

### Hall Classification

_weight=0.30
):
    """
    Palace-mode retrieval: navigate by hall first, fall back to full search.

    The palace insight: don't search everything flat. Enter through the right
    hall — a smaller, more focused subset — and get a tight answer fast.
    Only widen to the full haystack if the hall search doesn't yield confidence.

    PALACE
      └── HALL (classified per session: preferences / facts / events / assistant / general)
            └── CLOSET (user turns per session — what the user said)
                  └── DRAWER (assistant turns — only opened for assistant-reference questions)
            └── PREFERENCE WING (synthetic docs from pref extraction — same session ID)

pus →     ROOM: diary                      14 drawers → →   WING: wing_ignis →     ROOM: diary                       5 drawers → →   WING: wing_session-hook →     ROOM: diary                      76 drawers → → ======================================================= **132,601 drawers** in the palace now. That's a massive knowledge base — every conversation across all your projects is searchable.

How It Works

### The Palace

The layout is fairly simple, though it took a long time to get there.

It starts with a **wing**. Every project, person, or topic you're filing gets its own wing in the palace.

Each wing has **rooms** connected to it, where information is divided into subjects that relate to that wing — so every room is a different element of what your project contains. Project ideas could be one room, employees could be another, financial statements another. There can be an endless number of rooms that split the wing into sections. The MemPalace install detects these for you automatically, and of course you can personalize it any way you feel is right.
Palace overview: total drawers, wing and room counts, AAAK spec, and memory protocol.

# Port 8085 already in use
If the daemon fails to start with `[Errno 98] address already in use`, it usually means a previous instance didn't shut down cleanly.

The included `palace-daemon.service` uses `ExecStartPre=-/usr/bin/fuser -k 8085/tcp` to automatically clear the port before starting. If running manually, you can clear it with:

    fuser -k 8085/tcp

free. The problem is ordering — when both start simultaneously, the launcher gets to :80 first with its child process.
